q: A Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ is called regular when:
text: A Noetherian local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ is called regular when: Regularity says the Zariski tangent-space dimension (embedding dimension) equals the Krull dimension. In general $\dim_k\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2\ge\dim A$, with equality iff $A$ is regular — the algebraic analogue of smoothness.

q: Let $(A,\mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with $M$ a f.g. $A$-module and $N\subseteq M$ a submodule. Arrange the steps in the proof that $\mathfrak{m}N=N\Rightarrow N=0$.
text: Let $(A,\mathfrak{m})$ be a Noetherian local ring with $M$ a f.g. $A$-module and $N\subseteq M$ a submodule. Arrange the steps in the proof that $\mathfrak{m}N=N\Rightarrow N=0$. The standard proof: $\mathfrak{m}N=N$ gives a matrix equation $Mn=n$ (Cayley–Hamilton trick), yielding $p(1)=0$ for a monic $p\in 1+\mathfrak{m}[t]$. Since $p(1)\equiv 1\pmod{\mathfrak{m}}$, it is a unit in $A$, so $N=0$. Option C describes a corollary, not the root argument. Look for a polynomial relation whose constant term is a unit modulo $\mathfrak{m}$.

q: Let $A=k[[t]]$ (formal power series), $\mathfrak{m}=(t)$. What is $\dim_k \mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4$?
text: Let $A=k[[t]]$ (formal power series), $\mathfrak{m}=(t)$. What is $\dim_k \mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4$? $\mathfrak{m}^n=(t^n)$ in $k[[t]]$, so $\mathfrak{m}^3/\mathfrak{m}^4\cong (t^3)/(t^4)\cong k$ as a $k$-vector space, spanned by the image of $t^3$. Dimension is $1$.

q: For a regular local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ of dimension $d$, which statement about the associated graded ring $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A=\bigoplus_{n\ge 0}\mathfrak{m}^n/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}$ is true?
text: For a regular local ring $(A,\mathfrak{m},k)$ of dimension $d$, which statement about the associated graded ring $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A=\bigoplus_{n\ge 0}\mathfrak{m}^n/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}$ is true? Regularity implies $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A$ is a polynomial ring: choosing a regular system of parameters $x_1,\dots,x_d$ (a $k$-basis of $\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2$) gives $\mathrm{gr}_\mathfrak{m}A\cong k[x_1,\dots,x_d]$. The formal completion $\hat A\cong k[[x_1,\dots,x_d]]$ (not the associated graded).

q: Which of the following is an integral extension?
text: Which of the following is an integral extension? $\sqrt{-5}$ satisfies the monic equation $t^2+5=0$ over $\mathbb{Z}$, making $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ integral over $\mathbb{Z}$. Localizations $\mathbb{Z}[1/2]$, $\mathbb{Q}$, and $k(x)$ invert non-unit elements, producing non-integral transcendental/fractional elements like $1/2$, which satisfies no monic $\mathbb{Z}$-polynomial.

q: By Noether normalization, $k[x,y]/(xy-1)$ is finite over $k[z]$ for some transcendental $z$. What is the minimum possible degree $[B:k[z]]$ of this finite extension (where $B=k[x,y]/(xy-1)$)?
text: By Noether normalization, $k[x,y]/(xy-1)$ is finite over $k[z]$ for some transcendental $z$. What is the minimum possible degree $[B:k[z]]$ of this finite extension (where $B=k[x,y]/(xy-1)$)? The hyperbola $xy=1$ has dimension $1$, so Noether normalization gives a finite map to $k[z]$ (one variable). Choosing $z=x+y$ works: then $x,y$ are the roots of $t^2-zt+1=0$ over $k[z]$, so $B$ is free of rank $2$ over $k[z]$.

q: If $A\subseteq B$ is an integral extension of domains, which fails?
text: If $A\subseteq B$ is an integral extension of domains, which fails? Integral does not imply finitely generated: $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ is integral over $\mathbb{Z}$ (every algebraic integer) but not f.g. The other statements are standard consequences of lying-over, going-up, and incomparability.

q: Let $A\subseteq B$ be an integral extension of Noetherian domains with $A$ integrally closed. If $\mathfrak{q}_1,\mathfrak{q}_2\subset B$ both lie over the same prime $\mathfrak{p}=\mathfrak{q}_i\cap A$, which must hold?
text: Let $A\subseteq B$ be an integral extension of Noetherian domains with $A$ integrally closed. If $\mathfrak{q}_1,\mathfrak{q}_2\subset B$ both lie over the same prime $\mathfrak{p}=\mathfrak{q}_i\cap A$, which must hold? By the theorem of going-up and incomparability, primes lying over $\mathfrak{p}$ are incomparable. When $B/A$ arises from a Galois extension of fraction fields, the Galois group acts transitively on the fiber $\{\mathfrak{q}:A\cap\mathfrak{q}=\mathfrak{p}\}$, so any two are conjugate. In general they need not be equal (e.g. $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ at $p=2$).

q: Let $B=\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (Gaussian integers) and $A=\mathbb{Z}$. How many prime ideals of $B$ lie over the prime $(5)\subset\mathbb{Z}$?
text: Let $B=\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (Gaussian integers) and $A=\mathbb{Z}$. How many prime ideals of $B$ lie over the prime $(5)\subset\mathbb{Z}$? $5=(2+i)(2-i)$ splits in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ since $5\equiv 1\pmod 4$. The two factors generate distinct prime ideals $(2+i)$ and $(2-i)$ in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$, both lying over $(5)$. So there are $2$ primes above $(5)$.

q: In the proof of Noether normalization for $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]/I$ over $k$ (infinite field), what is the role of the linear change of variables $x_i\mapsto x_i-\lambda_i x_n$?
text: In the proof of Noether normalization for $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]/I$ over $k$ (infinite field), what is the role of the linear change of variables $x_i\mapsto x_i-\lambda_i x_n$? Given a nonzero $f\in I$, the substitution $x_i\mapsto x_i+\lambda_i x_n$ (generic $\lambda_i\in k$) makes the leading monomial in $x_n$ have a nonzero coefficient, so $f$ becomes monic (after scaling) in $x_n$. This exhibits $x_n$ as integral over $k[x_1,\dots,x_{n-1}]$, allowing induction on $n$.

q: What is the Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
text: What is the Krull dimension of $k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$? Over a field, $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$. Chain: $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)$.

q: What is $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$?
text: What is $\dim k[x,y]/(xy)$? $\mathrm{Spec}\,k[x,y]/(xy)$ is the union of the two coordinate axes. The longest chain of primes is (generic point of one axis) $\subsetneq$ (origin), giving dimension $1$.

q: What is $\dim \mathbb{Z}[x]$?
text: What is $\dim \mathbb{Z}[x]$? For Noetherian $A$, $\dim A[x]=\dim A+1$. Since $\dim \mathbb{Z}=1$, we get $\dim \mathbb{Z}[x]=2$. A chain: $(0)\subsetneq (p)\subsetneq (p,x)$.

q: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (the twisted cubic curve).
text: Compute $\dim k[x,y,z]/(xz-y^2, yz-x^3, z^2-x^2 y)$ (the twisted cubic curve). The twisted cubic is an irreducible curve in $\mathbb{A}^3$ parametrized by $t\mapsto(t,t^2,t^3)$. The ideal cuts out a $1$-dimensional variety (a curve), so the Krull dimension of the quotient ring is $1$. A prime chain: $(0)\subsetneq\mathfrak{m}$ for any point ideal $\mathfrak{m}$.

q: Which is an example of a Noetherian ring that is NOT catenary (where catenary means all maximal chains between two primes have the same length)?
text: Which is an example of a Noetherian ring that is NOT catenary (where catenary means all maximal chains between two primes have the same length)? Nagata constructed a Noetherian domain with two maximal ideals $\mathfrak{m}_1,\mathfrak{m}_2$ such that all saturated chains from $(0)$ to $\mathfrak{m}_1$ have length $1$ but all chains to $\mathfrak{m}_2$ have length $2$. Polynomial rings over fields, $\mathbb{Z}$, and fields are all catenary (universally catenary, in fact).

q: Let $A=k[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$ (coordinate ring of the $2$-sphere over $k=\mathbb{R}$). Compute $\dim A$.
text: Let $A=k[x,y,z]/(x^2+y^2+z^2-1)$ (coordinate ring of the $2$-sphere over $k=\mathbb{R}$). Compute $\dim A$. The $2$-sphere is an irreducible $2$-dimensional variety, so $\dim A = 2$. More precisely, $A$ is a domain of transcendence degree $2$ over $k$ (the sphere maps finitely to $\mathbb{A}^2$ via projection), giving Krull dimension $2$.

title: Chain complexes and homology
blurb: Complexes encode algebraic data with differentials, and homology measures failure of exactness.

title: Exact sequences
blurb: Exactness at B means im f = ker g; short exact sequences 0→A→B→C→0 are the atomic building blocks of homological algebra.

title: The snake lemma
blurb: A commutative diagram with exact rows yields a connecting map δ:ker γ → coker α and a six-term exact sequence linking kernels to cokernels.

title: Long exact sequence in homology
blurb: A short exact sequence of chain complexes induces a long exact sequence in homology, built by iterating the snake-lemma connecting map in each degree.

title: Derived functors and resolutions
blurb: Projective or injective resolutions define Ext and Tor as computable invariants.

title: Spectral sequences
blurb: Spectral sequences stage difficult computations into successive approximation pages.

q: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying:
text: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying: The defining condition of a chain complex is $\partial^2=0$, i.e. $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. This guarantees $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq \ker\partial_n$, so $H_n=\ker\partial_n/\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$ is well-defined.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$?
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$? The triangle is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$, so $H_0=\mathbb{Q}$ and $H_1=\mathbb{Q}$. Its one independent $1$-cycle is the loop around the triangle.

q: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$.
text: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$. $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$. Every closed orientable surface of genus $g$ has $\chi=2-2g$; here $g=1$.

q: Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$?
text:
Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$? We need $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. For option B: $0\circ 2=0$. For A: $3\circ 2=6
e 0$. For C: $\mathrm{id}\circ\mathrm{id}=\mathrm{id}
e 0$. For D: $2\circ 3=6
e 0$.

q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$).
text: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$). $H_0=\ker(C_{-1}\to C_{-2})/\operatorname{im}(\partial_1)=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$, which has order $3$.

q: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition?
text: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition? A chain map must commute with the boundary maps: $\partial^D\circ f=f\circ\partial^C$. This ensures $f$ descends to a well-defined map on homology groups.

q: A sequence $A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C$ is exact at $B$ when:
text: A sequence $A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C$ is exact at $B$ when: Exactness at $B$ is the equality $\operatorname{im} f = \ker g$. The weaker condition $g\circ f=0$ only gives $\operatorname{im} f\subseteq\ker g$; the reverse inclusion is the substantive content.

q: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct?
text: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct? $\operatorname{im}(\cdot n)=n\mathbb{Z}=\ker(\text{mod }n)$, so it is short exact. A splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/n\to\mathbb{Z}$, and there is none for $n\ge 2$ (any image is torsion-free). So it splits iff $n=1$, where the sequence is trivial.

q: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$.
text: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$. Orders multiply across a short exact sequence: $|B|=|A|\cdot|C|=6\cdot 10=60$. This is the group-theoretic analogue of $\dim B = \dim A + \dim C$ for vector spaces.

q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split?
text: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split? $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ is short exact but does not split: a splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$, which is impossible since $\mathbb{Z}$ is torsion-free.

q: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$?
text: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$? $\partial:\mathbb{Z}^2\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}^3$ is injective (since $H_1(B)=0$ forces exactness). Its cokernel has rank $3-2=1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is:
text: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is: The five-lemma: with exact rows and the four outer verticals being isomorphisms, a standard diagram chase (injectivity first, then surjectivity) proves the middle map is also an isomorphism. Two diagram chases — injectivity then surjectivity — both ride on the outer isomorphisms.

q: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target?
text: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target? The snake connects $\ker\gamma$ (on the right of the top row) to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ (on the left of the bottom row) via the recipe lift $\to$ $\beta$ $\to$ pull back along $i'$.

q: Which six-term exact sequence does the snake lemma produce?
text: Which six-term exact sequence does the snake lemma produce? Kernels first (reading left-to-right), then $\delta$, then cokernels (again left-to-right). The 0 on the left encodes 'left-most kernel arrow is injective' and the 0 on the right encodes 'right-most cokernel arrow is surjective'.

q: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$?
text: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$? Lift $c=1$ to $b=1\in\mathbb{Z}/4$; $\beta(b)=2$; pull back along $i':a'\mapsto 2a'$ to get $a'=1\in\mathbb{Z}/2$. Since $\operatorname{im}\alpha=0$, $\delta(1)=1$.

q: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$?
text: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$? The snake recipe: (1) lift $c$ to some $b\in B$ using surjectivity from $j$, (2) compute $\beta(b)\in B'$, (3) since $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(j(b))=\gamma(c)=0$, exactness gives $\beta(b)=i'(a')$ for a unique $a'\in A'$, (4) $\delta(c)=[a']\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Independence of lift follows from $\partial^2=0$.

q: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically?
text: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically? If $\alpha$ is surjective then $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, so $\delta$ must be the zero map. Alternatively, if $\gamma$ is injective then $\ker\gamma=0$ and $\delta$ has trivial domain.

q: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$?
text: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$? With all vertical maps being identities, $\ker\gamma=0$ (since $\gamma=\mathrm{id}$, every element is in the image, kernel is trivial — but actually $\ker(\mathrm{id})=0$). Wait: $\ker\delta$ is the kernel of $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Since $\ker\gamma=0$ (identity is injective), the domain is $0$ and $\ker\delta=0$. But if rows are $0\to\mathbb{Z}/4\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}}\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$, then $\ker\gamma=\mathbb{Z}/4$, $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, and $\delta=0$ with $\ker\delta=\mathbb{Z}/4$, so $|\ker\delta|=4$.

q: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it.
text: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it. Step 4 has the bug. Defining $\delta(c)=a'$ in $A'$ itself is not well-defined because the lift $b$ was a choice, and $a'$ depends on that choice by an element of $\operatorname{im}\alpha$ (as step 5 correctly notes). The fix is exactly what step 5 sets up: define $\delta(c)=[a']\in A'/\operatorname{im}\alpha=\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, which then is well-defined. Compare target spaces: $\delta$ must land in a quotient, not in $A'$ itself.

q: A short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$ induces a long exact sequence whose connecting map $\partial$ has which degree?
text: A short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$ induces a long exact sequence whose connecting map $\partial$ has which degree? The snake lemma applied degree-by-degree produces $\partial:H_n(C)\to H_{n-1}(A)$, lowering homological degree by 1 (for a cohomologically indexed complex the analogous map raises degree by 1).

q: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$.
text: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$. Covering $S^n$ by two contractible hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^{n-1}$, the MV sequence gives $H_n(S^n)\cong H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})$ for $n\ge 2$, and $H_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ directly. Inductively $\dim H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})=1$.

q: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to:
text: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to: With the middle terms $H_n(B)=H_{n-1}(B)=0$ the sequence $0\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to 0$ forces $\partial$ to be an isomorphism. Acyclic complexes are 'invisible' except for the degree shift they impose.

q: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct?
text: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct? A cycle $z$ on $X$ can be written $z=a+b$ (with chains supported in $A$ and $B$ respectively). Then $\partial a = -\partial b$ is a cycle in $A\cap B$; its homology class is $\partial[z]$. This is the snake-lemma connecting map applied to the Mayer–Vietoris short exact sequence of chain complexes.

q: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$. The LES gives $H_1(D^2)\to H_1(D^2,S^1)\to H_0(S^1)\to H_0(D^2)$. Since $D^2$ is contractible, $H_1(D^2)=0$ and the map $H_0(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}\to H_0(D^2)\cong\mathbb{Q}$ is an isomorphism. So $H_1(D^2,S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$, dimension $1$. (By excision this equals $\tilde{H}_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$.)

q: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence?
text: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence? Naturality is proved by chasing the snake-lemma diagram for the naturality square: a map of short exact sequences of chain complexes induces a map of long exact sequences, and the connecting homomorphisms commute with the induced maps because each step of the snake construction is natural.

q: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is:
text: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is: Right derived functors of a left exact $F$ are computed from an injective resolution $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$, applying $F$ and taking cohomology of $F(I^\bullet)$. The answer is independent of the resolution.

q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
text: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$. From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, dimension $1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

q: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$.
text: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$. $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(6,4)=2$.

q: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is:
text: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot p}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/p\to 0$ is a free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$ of length $1$. Applying $\operatorname{Hom}(-,M)$ and taking cohomology gives $\operatorname{Ext}^0=M^p$ (fixed by $p$) and $\operatorname{Ext}^1=M/pM$; higher Ext groups vanish.

q: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group).
text: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group). $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(12,8)=4$, so the group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$ with order $4$.

q: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)?
text: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)? $\operatorname{Hom}_R(M,-)$ is left exact but not right exact in general: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ applied with $M=\mathbb{Z}$ gives $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$, which fails surjectivity. Its right derived functors are the $\operatorname{Ext}^n$ groups.

q: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree:
text: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree: In the standard cohomological convention $d_r:E_r^{p,q}\to E_r^{p+r,q-r+1}$, i.e. bidegree $(r,1-r)$. The next page is defined as $E_{r+1}=H(E_r,d_r)$.

q: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if:
text: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if: Degeneration at $E_2$ means $d_r=0$ for all $r\ge 2$, so $E_\infty=E_2$. The abutment $H^n$ is then the associated graded of some filtration with graded pieces $E_2^{p,n-p}$ — you still have an extension problem, but the entries are pinned down.

q: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group?
text: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group? The horizontal map $\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$ is injective, so its kernel is $0$. Thus $E_1^{0,0}=\ker(2)=0$, which has rank $0$ (and is in fact the zero group).

q: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$?
text: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$? $H^p(S^2)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $p=0,2$ and $0$ otherwise. $H^q(S^1)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $q=0,1$ and $0$ otherwise. So the only nonzero corners of the $E_2$ page are $(p,q)\in\{(0,0),(2,0),(0,1),(2,1)\}$.

q: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is:
text: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is: For the Hopf fibration $S^1\to S^3\to S^2$, the spectral sequence must give $H^*(S^3)$: $\mathbb{Z}$ in degrees $0$ and $3$. To kill $E_\infty^{0,1}$ and $E_\infty^{2,0}$ we need $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\cong\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_2^{2,0}\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to be an isomorphism.

q: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$.
text: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$. Degeneration gives $H^n(E)\cong\bigoplus_{p+q=n}H^p(B)\otimes H^q(F)$. For $n=2$: $(p,q)=(2,0)$ contributes $H^2(S^2)\otimes H^0(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$, and $(0,2)$ contributes $H^0(S^2)\otimes H^2(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$. Total $\dim=2$.

title: Real numbers
blurb: The complete ordered field: least-upper-bound property, sequences and Cauchy completeness, and why Q isn't enough.

title: Continuity on R
blurb: ε–δ continuity, IVT, EVT, and the interaction between continuity and compactness on intervals.

title: Differentiation
blurb: The derivative as best linear approximation, chain rule, mean value theorem, and Taylor's theorem with remainder.

title: Uniform convergence
blurb: Uniform vs pointwise convergence of function sequences, Weierstrass M-test, and why uniform limits preserve continuity and integrability.

title: Riemann integral
blurb: Upper/lower sums, the fundamental theorem of calculus, and the failure modes that motivate measure theory.

title: Multivariable differentiation
blurb: Partial derivatives, total derivative as Jacobian, chain rule for vector-valued maps, gradients and directional derivatives.

title: Multivariable integration
blurb: Iterated integrals, Fubini's theorem, Jacobian determinant and the change-of-variables formula on R^n.

title: Inverse & implicit function theorems
blurb: Local invertibility from invertible Jacobian; solving F(x,y)=0 for y locally when ∂F/∂y is invertible — the engine behind manifolds and submanifolds.

q: Which property distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Which property distinguishes $\mathbb{R}$ from $\mathbb{Q}$? $\mathbb{Q}$ is an ordered, Archimedean field, and closed under addition. The distinguishing property is the least-upper-bound (Dedekind) completeness: the set $\{q\in\mathbb{Q}:q^2<2\}$ is bounded above but has no supremum in $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: Let $S = \{1 - 1/n : n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\}\subset\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sup S$?
text: Let $S = \{1 - 1/n : n\in\mathbb{N}_{\ge 1}\}\subset\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sup S$? $S$ is bounded above by $1$ and its elements approach $1$. Since $1$ is an upper bound and no smaller number is, $\sup S = 1$. Note $1\notin S$ — the supremum need not be attained.

q: Which statement is equivalent to $\mathbb{R}$ being Cauchy-complete?
text: Which statement is equivalent to $\mathbb{R}$ being Cauchy-complete? Cauchy completeness is exactly the statement that every Cauchy sequence has a limit in the space. Bounded sequences need not converge ($(-1)^n$); monotone-bounded sequences do, and bounded sequences have convergent subsequences (Bolzano–Weierstrass) — but those are consequences, not definitions.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to prove that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to prove that every Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ converges? Proof chain: (1) Every Cauchy sequence is bounded. (2) Bolzano–Weierstrass extracts a convergent subsequence $a_{n_k}\to L$. (3) For any $\varepsilon>0$ choose $N$ so $|a_m-a_n| N$ and $k$ with $n_k>N$ and $|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon/2$; then $|a_n-L|\le|a_n-a_{n_k}|+|a_{n_k}-L|<\varepsilon$.

q: Let $A = \{x\in\mathbb{Q} : x^2 < 3\}$. In $\mathbb{R}$, $\sup A = \sqrt{3}$. How many rational numbers satisfy $x^2 = 3$?
text: Let $A = \{x\in\mathbb{Q} : x^2 < 3\}$. In $\mathbb{R}$, $\sup A = \sqrt{3}$. How many rational numbers satisfy $x^2 = 3$? $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational (its square is $3$, but $3$ is not a perfect square, and a standard argument shows $\sqrt{3}\notin\mathbb{Q}$). So there are $0$ rational solutions to $x^2=3$, illustrating why $\sup A$ is not attained in $\mathbb{Q}$.

q: If $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, which theorem guarantees a root of $f$?
text: If $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(0)=-1$ and $f(1)=2$, which theorem guarantees a root of $f$? IVT: a continuous function on $[a,b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. Since $0$ lies between $-1$ and $2$, some $c\in[0,1]$ has $f(c)=0$.

q: For $f(x)=2x+3$ and $a=1$, with $\varepsilon=0.01$, what is the largest $\delta$ that works in the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of continuity?
text: For $f(x)=2x+3$ and $a=1$, with $\varepsilon=0.01$, what is the largest $\delta$ that works in the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition of continuity? $|f(x)-f(1)| = |2x+3 - 5| = 2|x-1|$. We need $2|x-1| < 0.01$, i.e. $|x-1| < 0.005$. So $\delta = 0.005$.

q: Which of these is NOT guaranteed for a continuous $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$?
text: Which of these is NOT guaranteed for a continuous $f\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$? Continuity on a closed bounded interval gives EVT (attains max/min), uniform continuity (via compactness), and that the image is compact and connected (so a closed bounded interval). Differentiability is a strictly stronger condition — e.g., $f(x)=|x-1/2|$ is continuous on $[0,1]$ but not differentiable at $1/2$.

q: Which is a correct counterexample showing a function can be continuous at every irrational but discontinuous at every rational?
text: Which is a correct counterexample showing a function can be continuous at every irrational but discontinuous at every rational? Thomae's function is continuous at every irrational (the set $\{x : f(x)\ge 1/n\}$ is finite for each $n$, so rationals with large $q$ are 'sparse near any irrational') and discontinuous at every rational (every neighborhood of $p/q$ contains irrationals where $f=0\ne 1/q$). This shows discontinuity sets need not be closed. You need a function whose "spikes" shrink as denominators grow.

q: Let $f(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)$ for $x\ne 0$ and $f(0)=0$. Using the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition with $\varepsilon=10^{-4}$, what is the largest $\delta$ guaranteeing $|f(x)-f(0)|<\varepsilon$ for $|x|<\delta$? (Hint: $|f(x)|\le x^2$.)
text: Let $f(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)$ for $x\ne 0$ and $f(0)=0$. Using the $\varepsilon$–$\delta$ definition with $\varepsilon=10^{-4}$, what is the largest $\delta$ guaranteeing $|f(x)-f(0)|<\varepsilon$ for $|x|<\delta$? (Hint: $|f(x)|\le x^2$.) $|f(x)-0|=|x^2\sin(1/x)|\le x^2 < \varepsilon$ whenever $|x|<\sqrt{\varepsilon}=\sqrt{10^{-4}}=0.01$. So $\delta=0.01$.

q: You are proving the intermediate value theorem: if $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(a)<0<f(b)$, then some $c\in(a,b)$ has $f(c)=0$. The steps so far use the nested-interval bisection idea.
text: You are proving the intermediate value theorem: if $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous with $f(a)<0<f(b)$, then some $c\in(a,b)$ has $f(c)=0$. The steps so far use the nested-interval bisection idea. The clean continuation uses continuity at $c$ to rule out $f(c) 0$ (points just before $c$ would be in $S$ with $f\ge 0$, same contradiction). So $f(c)=0$. The sup must be a root because each side of $f(c)\ne 0$ pushes $c$ off the supremum.

q: If $f(x)=x^3$ on $[0,3]$, find $c\in(0,3)$ given by the mean value theorem: $f'(c) = (f(3)-f(0))/3$.
text: If $f(x)=x^3$ on $[0,3]$, find $c\in(0,3)$ given by the mean value theorem: $f'(c) = (f(3)-f(0))/3$. Average slope $= (27-0)/3 = 9$. Set $3c^2 = 9$, so $c = \sqrt 3 \approx 1.7320508$.

q: The derivative $f'(a)$ is best interpreted as:
text: The derivative $f'(a)$ is best interpreted as: The derivative is the best-linear-approximation characterization: $f'(a)$ is the (scalar) coefficient of the unique linear $L\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ for which the remainder is little-o of $|h|$. This is the formulation that generalizes to the Jacobian in several variables.

q: The 2nd-degree Taylor polynomial of $f(x)=\cos x$ at $a=0$ is $1 - x^2/2$. What is the Lagrange remainder bound for $|x|\le 0.1$? (Use $\max|f'''(\xi)|\le 1$.)
text: The 2nd-degree Taylor polynomial of $f(x)=\cos x$ at $a=0$ is $1 - x^2/2$. What is the Lagrange remainder bound for $|x|\le 0.1$? (Use $\max|f'''(\xi)|\le 1$.) Lagrange: $|R_2(x)| \le \max|f^{(3)}(\xi)| \cdot |x|^3/3! \le 1 \cdot (0.1)^3/6 = 0.001/6 \approx 0.000166\overline{6}$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Mean Value Theorem applied to $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ differentiable on $(a,b)$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Mean Value Theorem applied to $f\colon[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ differentiable on $(a,b)$? The standard proof introduces the auxiliary function $g$ that measures deviation from the secant line. $g(a)=g(b)=0$ satisfies Rolle's hypothesis (continuous on $[a,b]$, differentiable on $(a,b)$). Rolle gives $c$ with $g'(c)=0$, which unpacks to $f'(c)=(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)$.

q: Let $f(x)=e^x$ on $[0,1]$. The Taylor remainder after the first-degree polynomial at $a=0$ is $R_1(x)=e^\xi x^2/2$ for some $\xi\in(0,x)$. Give the Lagrange upper bound for $|R_1(1)|$ using $e^\xi\le e^1\approx 2.71828$.
text: Let $f(x)=e^x$ on $[0,1]$. The Taylor remainder after the first-degree polynomial at $a=0$ is $R_1(x)=e^\xi x^2/2$ for some $\xi\in(0,x)$. Give the Lagrange upper bound for $|R_1(1)|$ using $e^\xi\le e^1\approx 2.71828$. $|R_1(1)|\le e^1\cdot 1^2/2 = e/2 \approx 2.71828/2 \approx 1.35914$.

q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Which statement is correct?
text: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Which statement is correct? For $x\in[0,1)$, $x^n\to 0$; at $x=1$, $f_n(1)=1\to 1$. Pointwise limit is discontinuous. Since $\sup_{[0,1]}|f_n-f|=1$ for every $n$, convergence is not uniform — uniform convergence preserves continuity.

q: Apply the Weierstrass M-test to $\sum_{n\ge 1} \sin(nx)/n^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty M_n$ with the optimal $M_n = 1/n^2$? (Give the numerical value.)
text: Apply the Weierstrass M-test to $\sum_{n\ge 1} \sin(nx)/n^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$. What is $\sum_{n=1}^\infty M_n$ with the optimal $M_n = 1/n^2$? (Give the numerical value.) $|\sin(nx)/n^2|\le 1/n^2 = M_n$. The series $\sum 1/n^2 = \pi^2/6 \approx 1.6449$ is finite, so the Weierstrass M-test gives absolute and uniform convergence on $\mathbb{R}$.

q: Suppose $f_n\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ are continuous and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Which is TRUE?
text: Suppose $f_n\colon[0,1]\to\mathbb{R}$ are continuous and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Which is TRUE? Uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous (standard $\varepsilon/3$ argument), and uniform convergence on $[a,b]$ lets you swap limit and Riemann integral. Uniform convergence does NOT in general preserve differentiability — additional hypotheses are needed there.

q: Which chain of implications is correct for sequences of functions on $[0,1]$?
text: Which chain of implications is correct for sequences of functions on $[0,1]$? Uniform convergence is strictly stronger: $\|f_n-f\|_\infty\to 0$ forces pointwise convergence. The converse fails — $f_n(x)=x^n$ converges pointwise to the discontinuous indicator of $\{1\}$, but $\sup|f_n-f|=1$ for all $n$.

q: The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-r, r]$ for $0<r<1$. Using the Weierstrass M-test with $M_n = r^n/n$, compute $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ at $r=1/2$. (Give the exact decimal value to 4 places.)
text: The series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{n} x^n$ converges uniformly on $[-r, r]$ for $0<r<1$. Using the Weierstrass M-test with $M_n = r^n/n$, compute $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n$ at $r=1/2$. (Give the exact decimal value to 4 places.) $\sum_{n=1}^\infty (1/2)^n/n = -\ln(1-1/2) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. The M-test bound is $\ln 2$, confirming absolute and uniform convergence on $[-1/2,\,1/2]$.

q: Which function is NOT Riemann-integrable on $[0,1]$?
text: Which function is NOT Riemann-integrable on $[0,1]$? Continuous functions, monotone functions, and bounded functions discontinuous on a measure-zero set are all Riemann-integrable (Lebesgue's criterion). The Dirichlet indicator is discontinuous everywhere in $[0,1]$, and its upper sum is always $1$, lower sum always $0$ — not Riemann-integrable.

q: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^2 (3x^2 - 1)\,dx$.
text: Using FTC, compute $\int_1^2 (3x^2 - 1)\,dx$. An antiderivative is $F(x) = x^3 - x$. FTC gives $F(2) - F(1) = (8-2) - (1-1) = 6$.

q: For $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$ and the uniform partition $P_n$ with $n$ subintervals, the gap $U(f,P_n)-L(f,P_n)$ equals $1/n$. What is this gap at $n=10$?
text: For $f(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$ and the uniform partition $P_n$ with $n$ subintervals, the gap $U(f,P_n)-L(f,P_n)$ equals $1/n$. What is this gap at $n=10$? On $[(k-1)/n, k/n]$, $\sup f - \inf f = 1/n$. Summing: $U - L = \sum_{k=1}^n (1/n)\cdot(1/n) = 1/n$. At $n=10$, gap $= 0.1$.

q: Which correctly orders the steps to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$?
text: Which correctly orders the steps to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1): if $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$, then $F(x)=\int_a^x f(t)\,dt$ satisfies $F'(x)=f(x)$? The clean proof uses the MVT for integrals: $\frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}{h}=\frac{1}{h}\int_x^{x+h}f(t)\,dt=f(c_h)$ where $c_h$ lies between $x$ and $x+h$. As $h\to 0$, $c_h\to x$, and continuity of $f$ gives $f(c_h)\to f(x)$.

q: Let $f(x) = \begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\ne 0 \\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}$. Is $f$ Riemann-integrable on $[-1,1]$? Compute $\int_{-1}^1 |f(x)|\,dx$ to 4 decimal places. (Hint: the integral equals $2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.)
text: Let $f(x) = \begin{cases}x\sin(1/x) & x\ne 0 \\ 0 & x=0\end{cases}$. Is $f$ Riemann-integrable on $[-1,1]$? Compute $\int_{-1}^1 |f(x)|\,dx$ to 4 decimal places. (Hint: the integral equals $2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.) $f$ is bounded and continuous except at $0$ (a single point, measure zero), so it is Riemann-integrable by Lebesgue's criterion. Numerically, $\int_{-1}^1 |x\sin(1/x)|\,dx=2\int_0^1 x|\sin(1/x)|\,dx\approx 0.4538$.

q: For $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the total derivative $DF(a)$ is:
text: For $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^m$, the total derivative $DF(a)$ is: $DF(a)$ is the best linear approximation — a linear map from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$. In the standard bases it is the Jacobian matrix $[\partial F^i/\partial x_j]$. For scalar-valued $F$ ($m=1$) the Jacobian is a row vector, not a scalar.

q: Let $f(x,y)=x^2 y + y^3$. Compute the directional derivative of $f$ at $(1,1)$ in the unit direction $v = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$.
text: Let $f(x,y)=x^2 y + y^3$. Compute the directional derivative of $f$ at $(1,1)$ in the unit direction $v = (1/\sqrt{2}, 1/\sqrt{2})$. $\nabla f = (2xy,\; x^2 + 3y^2)$. At $(1,1)$: $\nabla f = (2, 4)$. Then $D_v f = (2,4)\cdot(1/\sqrt 2, 1/\sqrt 2) = 6/\sqrt{2} = 3\sqrt{2} \approx 4.2426$.

q: Existence of all partial derivatives of $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ at $a$ implies:
text: Existence of all partial derivatives of $f\colon\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}$ at $a$ implies: Bare existence of partials is too weak. The standard counterexample $f(x,y) = xy/(x^2+y^2)$ (with $f(0,0)=0$) has both partials at the origin yet fails to be continuous — let alone differentiable — there. Continuity of the partials in a neighborhood is the standard sufficient condition.

q: Which is a valid counterexample showing that existence of all directional derivatives at a point does NOT imply differentiability?
text: Which is a valid counterexample showing that existence of all directional derivatives at a point does NOT imply differentiability? Along any ray $(t\cos\theta, t\sin\theta)$, the function $f$ tends to $0$ as $t\to 0$, so all directional derivatives exist and equal $0$. But along the parabolic curve $y=x^2$, $f(x,x^2)=1/2\not\to 0$. Thus $f$ is not continuous (let alone differentiable) at the origin. Straight lines see zero; try a parabolic path into the origin instead.

q: Let $f(x,y)=3x^2+2xy+y^2$. Compute the mixed partial $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x$ at any point.
text: Let $f(x,y)=3x^2+2xy+y^2$. Compute the mixed partial $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x$ at any point. $\partial f/\partial x = 6x+2y$. Then $\partial^2 f/\partial y\,\partial x = \partial(6x+2y)/\partial y = 2$. By Clairaut's theorem this equals $\partial^2 f/\partial x\,\partial y=2$ (both partials are continuous).

q: Use polar coordinates $(x,y) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ to evaluate $\iint_D 1\,dA$, where $D$ is the disk $\{x^2+y^2\le 4\}$.
text: Use polar coordinates $(x,y) = (r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta)$ to evaluate $\iint_D 1\,dA$, where $D$ is the disk $\{x^2+y^2\le 4\}$. In polar, $dA = r\,dr\,d\theta$. The integral becomes $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^2 r\,dr\,d\theta = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566$. This is the area of the disk of radius $2$.

q: Fubini's theorem (Riemann form) lets you swap iterated integrals $\iint_{[a,b]\times[c,d]} f$ when:
text: Fubini's theorem (Riemann form) lets you swap iterated integrals $\iint_{[a,b]\times[c,d]} f$ when: Fubini in the Riemann setting requires integrability on the box plus integrability of slices. Continuity of $f$ is the usual sufficient hypothesis. Tonelli's theorem handles nonnegative measurable functions in the Lebesgue setting without requiring a priori integrability.

q: Under the change of variables $(u,v)\mapsto(2u,\,3v)$, by what factor does area on $\mathbb{R}^2$ scale?
text: Under the change of variables $(u,v)\mapsto(2u,\,3v)$, by what factor does area on $\mathbb{R}^2$ scale? The Jacobian matrix is $\operatorname{diag}(2,3)$, with determinant $|\det J| = 6$. Areas scale by exactly this factor.

q: Fubini's theorem can fail for signed integrands. Which is the standard counterexample showing $\int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \ne \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$?
text: Fubini's theorem can fail for signed integrands. Which is the standard counterexample showing $\int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dx\,dy \ne \int_0^1\int_0^1 f(x,y)\,dy\,dx$? Fubini requires $\iint|f|<\infty$. For $f=(x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^2$ on $[0,1]^2$ the double integral of $|f|$ diverges, so Tonelli's condition fails and the two iterated integrals differ by a sign, giving $\pi/4$ vs $-\pi/4$. Look for an integrand where $\iint|f|$ diverges near the origin.

q: Compute $\iint_D x\,dA$ where $D$ is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ (i.e., $0\le y\le x\le 1$).
text: Compute $\iint_D x\,dA$ where $D$ is the triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, $(1,1)$ (i.e., $0\le y\le x\le 1$). Iterated: $\int_0^1\int_0^x x\,dy\,dx = \int_0^1 x\cdot x\,dx = \int_0^1 x^2\,dx = [x^3/3]_0^1 = 1/3\approx 0.33333$.

q: Let $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$. The inverse function theorem says $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $a$ when:
text: Let $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ be $C^1$. The inverse function theorem says $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $a$ when: IFT: if $F$ is $C^1$ on an open neighborhood of $a$ and the Jacobian $J_F(a)$ is invertible (equivalently, $\det J_F(a)\neq 0$), then $F$ restricts to a $C^1$-diffeomorphism between open neighborhoods of $a$ and $F(a)$.

q: On the unit circle $F(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$, at the point $(x_0, y_0) = (1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)$, implicit differentiation gives $dy/dx = -F_x/F_y$. Compute this slope.
text: On the unit circle $F(x,y) = x^2 + y^2 - 1 = 0$, at the point $(x_0, y_0) = (1/2, \sqrt{3}/2)$, implicit differentiation gives $dy/dx = -F_x/F_y$. Compute this slope. $F_x = 2x = 1$, $F_y = 2y = \sqrt{3}$. Then $dy/dx = -1/\sqrt{3} = -\sqrt{3}/3 \approx -0.5774$. This is the slope of the tangent line to the unit circle at $(1/2,\sqrt 3/2)$.

q: The implicit function theorem fails to guarantee a local solution $y = g(x)$ of $F(x,y) = 0$ precisely when:
text: The implicit function theorem fails to guarantee a local solution $y = g(x)$ of $F(x,y) = 0$ precisely when: The $y$-block Jacobian (here $\partial F/\partial y$) must be invertible. If $\partial F/\partial y(x_0, y_0) = 0$, IFT does not apply — geometrically, the tangent to $\{F=0\}$ is vertical, so the curve fails to be locally a graph $y = g(x)$ (e.g., the unit circle at $(\pm 1, 0)$).

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Inverse Function Theorem (IFT) for $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$, $C^1$, $\det J_F(a)\ne 0$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Inverse Function Theorem (IFT) for $F\colon\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$, $C^1$, $\det J_F(a)\ne 0$? Standard proof chain: (1) $\det J_F$ is continuous, so $J_F$ stays invertible near $a$. (2) Use the contraction mapping theorem (Banach fixed-point) on a suitable ball to produce a unique local inverse. (3) Differentiate $G(F(x))=x$ via the chain rule to get $DG(y)=(J_F(G(y)))^{-1}$, establishing $C^1$ regularity.

q: Let $F(x,y)=(x^2-y^2,\,2xy)$. Compute $|\det J_F(1,1)|$.
text: Let $F(x,y)=(x^2-y^2,\,2xy)$. Compute $|\det J_F(1,1)|$. $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2x & -2y \\ 2y & 2x\end{pmatrix}$. At $(1,1)$: $J_F=\begin{pmatrix}2&-2\\2&2\end{pmatrix}$. $\det J_F = (2)(2)-(-2)(2)=4+4=8$. Since $8\ne 0$, IFT guarantees $F$ is a local diffeomorphism near $(1,1)$.

title: σ-algebras and measures
blurb: σ-algebras, countably additive measures, and the Borel σ-algebra generated by the open sets of R.

title: Lebesgue measure
blurb: Outer measure, Carathéodory's construction, null sets, and why Lebesgue measure extends length from intervals to Borel sets.

title: Measurable functions
blurb: f measurable iff preimages of Borel sets are measurable; simple functions approximate measurable functions from below.

title: Lebesgue integral
blurb: Integrate by partitioning the range, not the domain; agrees with Riemann when Riemann exists, but handles far more functions.

title: Monotone and dominated convergence
blurb: MCT and DCT let you swap limit and integral under mild hypotheses — the machine behind most measure-theoretic arguments.

title: Lᵖ spaces
blurb: Banach spaces of p-th-power integrable functions, Hölder and Minkowski, L² as the canonical Hilbert space of analysis.

q: Which collection on a set $X$ is always a $\sigma$-algebra?
text: Which collection on a set $X$ is always a $\sigma$-algebra? $\{\varnothing, X\}$ is closed under complements and countable unions. Open sets and intervals are not closed under complements, and finite subsets are not closed under countable unions in an infinite set.

q: Let $\mu$ be a measure with $\mu(A)=3$, $\mu(B)=4$, and $\mu(A\cap B)=1$. Compute $\mu(A\cup B)$.
text: Let $\mu$ be a measure with $\mu(A)=3$, $\mu(B)=4$, and $\mu(A\cap B)=1$. Compute $\mu(A\cup B)$. Use finite additivity with overlap correction: $\mu(A\cup B)=\mu(A)+\mu(B)-\mu(A\cap B)=3+4-1=6$.

q: Write $e^{i\pi}$ as $a+bi$.
text: Write $e^{i\pi}$ as $a+bi$. Euler's formula gives $e^{i\theta}=\cos\theta+i\sin\theta$, so $e^{i\pi}=\cos\pi+i\sin\pi=-1+0i$.

q: Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the collection of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ that are either finite or have finite complement. Which property fails, showing $\mathcal{A}$ is NOT a $\sigma$-algebra?
text: Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the collection of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ that are either finite or have finite complement. Which property fails, showing $\mathcal{A}$ is NOT a $\sigma$-algebra? Take $A_n=\{2n\}$ for $n\ge 1$; each $A_n$ is finite so $A_n\in\mathcal{A}$. But $\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty A_n$ is the set of all even positive integers, which is infinite and has infinite complement (the odd naturals), so it is not in $\mathcal{A}$. Thus $\mathcal{A}$ fails closure under countable unions.

q: Let $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(X)=1$. Suppose $A_1\supset A_2\supset\cdots$ with $\mu(A_1)=0.8$ and $\mu(A_n)\to L$. If $\mu(A_3)=0.5$ and $\mu(A_5)=0.3$, and $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty A_n=\varnothing$, what is $L$?
text: Let $(X,\mathcal{A},\mu)$ be a measure space with $\mu(X)=1$. Suppose $A_1\supset A_2\supset\cdots$ with $\mu(A_1)=0.8$ and $\mu(A_n)\to L$. If $\mu(A_3)=0.5$ and $\mu(A_5)=0.3$, and $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty A_n=\varnothing$, what is $L$? By continuity of measure from above: if $\mu(A_1)<\infty$ and $A_n\downarrow\varnothing$, then $\mu(A_n)\to\mu(\varnothing)=0$. The values at $n=3,5$ are irrelevant to the limit.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all closed sets?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that the Borel $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ contains all closed sets? $\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$ is defined as the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all open sets. Since every closed set $F=U^c$ for some open $U$, and $\sigma$-algebras are closed under complementation, $F\in\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$.

q: What is the Lebesgue measure of a singleton $\{x\}\subset\mathbb{R}$?
text: What is the Lebesgue measure of a singleton $\{x\}\subset\mathbb{R}$? Points have zero length, so every singleton has Lebesgue measure $0$.

q: Compute $m([2,5])$, where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$.
text: Compute $m([2,5])$, where $m$ is Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$. Intervals are measured by length: $m([a,b])=b-a$, so $m([2,5])=5-2=3$.

q: Compute $(1+i)^2$.
text: Compute $(1+i)^2$. $(1+i)^2=1+2i+i^2=2i$, so the real part is $0$ and imaginary part is $2$.

q: The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds from $[0,1]$. Which statement correctly describes its Lebesgue measure and cardinality?
text: The Cantor set $C$ is constructed by removing middle thirds from $[0,1]$. Which statement correctly describes its Lebesgue measure and cardinality? At stage $n$, total removed length is $\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}2^k/3^{k+1}\to 1$, so $m(C)=0$. Yet $C$ is in bijection with $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ via ternary expansions, making it uncountable. This is the standard counterexample of an uncountable null set.

q: A Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ is non-measurable. Assuming translation-invariance, if $\{V+q:q\in\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]\}$ are pairwise disjoint and cover $[0,2]$, what value does $\sum_{q}m(V+q)$ take if $m(V)$ existed and were positive?
text: A Vitali set $V\subset[0,1]$ is non-measurable. Assuming translation-invariance, if $\{V+q:q\in\mathbb{Q}\cap[-1,1]\}$ are pairwise disjoint and cover $[0,2]$, what value does $\sum_{q}m(V+q)$ take if $m(V)$ existed and were positive? If $m(V)=c>0$, countable additivity gives $m([0,2])\ge\sum_{q}c=\infty$, contradicting $m([0,2])=2$. If $m(V)=0$, then $m([0,2])=0$, again a contradiction. So no consistent value of $m(V)$ exists; the correct answer reflecting the only non-contradictory finite value attempted is $0$, but in truth measurability fails entirely.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing outer regularity of Lebesgue measure: $m(E)=\inf\{m(U):U\supset E,\ U\text{ open}\}$?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for showing outer regularity of Lebesgue measure: $m(E)=\inf\{m(U):U\supset E,\ U\text{ open}\}$? For any $\varepsilon>0$, cover $E$ by open intervals $I_n$ with $\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$; their union $U=\bigcup I_n$ is open, contains $E$, and $m(U)\le\sum m(I_n)<m(E)+\varepsilon$. Since $m(U)\ge m(E)$ always, the infimum equals $m(E)$.

q: A function $f\colon(X,\mathcal A)\to(\mathbb R,\mathcal B)$ is measurable iff for every Borel set $B\subset\mathbb R$ we have:
text: A function $f\colon(X,\mathcal A)\to(\mathbb R,\mathcal B)$ is measurable iff for every Borel set $B\subset\mathbb R$ we have: Measurability is defined by preimages: all Borel sets pull back to measurable sets in $\mathcal A$.

q: Let $f=\mathbf 1_{[0,2]}$ on $\mathbb R$. What is $\int f\,dm$?
text: Let $f=\mathbf 1_{[0,2]}$ on $\mathbb R$. What is $\int f\,dm$? The indicator integral equals measure of the set: $\int \mathbf 1_E\,dm = m(E)$, so $m([0,2])=2$.

q: Compute $\overline{(3-4i)}$.
text: Compute $\overline{(3-4i)}$. Complex conjugation flips the sign of the imaginary part: $\overline{a+bi}=a-bi$.

q: Let $f_n\to f$ pointwise. Which is the correct counterexample showing that pointwise limits of measurable functions can be non-measurable if the $\sigma$-algebra is not complete?
text: Let $f_n\to f$ pointwise. Which is the correct counterexample showing that pointwise limits of measurable functions can be non-measurable if the $\sigma$-algebra is not complete? In an incomplete measure space, a null set $Z$ may contain non-measurable subsets $N$. Then $f_n=0$ (measurable) converges pointwise to $\mathbf{1}_N$, which is not measurable. Completeness is exactly the condition that prevents this: it ensures every subset of a null set is measurable.

q: Let $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be a simple function $f=3\cdot\mathbf{1}_A+7\cdot\mathbf{1}_B$ where $A=[0,1]$, $B=(2,4]$, and $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Compute $\int f\,dm$.
text: Let $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be a simple function $f=3\cdot\mathbf{1}_A+7\cdot\mathbf{1}_B$ where $A=[0,1]$, $B=(2,4]$, and $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Compute $\int f\,dm$. For a simple function $\int\sum c_i\mathbf{1}_{E_i}\,dm=\sum c_i m(E_i)$. So $\int f\,dm=3\cdot m([0,1])+7\cdot m((2,4])=3\cdot 1+7\cdot 2=3+14=17$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that $f+g$ is measurable when $f,g:(X,\mathcal{A})\to\mathbb{R}$ are measurable?
text: Which is the correct proof-step ordering for showing that $f+g$ is measurable when $f,g:(X,\mathcal{A})\to\mathbb{R}$ are measurable? The standard elementary proof: $f+g>a$ iff $f>a-g$ iff $\exists q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $f>q>a-g$, giving $\{f+g>a\}=\bigcup_{q\in\mathbb{Q}}(\{f>q\}\cap\{g>a-q\})$. Each factor is measurable; $\mathcal{A}$ is closed under finite intersections and countable unions.

q: For nonnegative measurable $f$, the Lebesgue integral is defined as:
text: For nonnegative measurable $f$, the Lebesgue integral is defined as: For $f\ge 0$, $\int f\,d\mu := \sup\{\int s\,d\mu : 0\le s\le f,\ s\text{ simple}\}$.

q: Compute $\int_0^1 x\,dx$ (Lebesgue measure).
text: Compute $\int_0^1 x\,dx$ (Lebesgue measure). Lebesgue and Riemann integrals agree here: $\int_0^1 x\,dx = [x^2/2]_0^1 = 1/2$.

q: Compute $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{ix}\,dx$ and report as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{ix}\,dx$ and report as $a+bi$. $e^{ix}=\cos x+i\sin x$. Both $\int_0^{2\pi}\cos x\,dx$ and $\int_0^{2\pi}\sin x\,dx$ are $0$, so the complex integral is $0+0i$.

q: Which function is Lebesgue integrable on $[0,1]$ but NOT Riemann integrable?
text: Which function is Lebesgue integrable on $[0,1]$ but NOT Riemann integrable? The Dirichlet function is nowhere continuous, so it is not Riemann integrable (upper and lower Riemann sums differ). However, $\mathbb{Q}\cap[0,1]$ is countable hence has Lebesgue measure $0$, so $\int_0^1\mathbf{1}_{\mathbb{Q}}\,dm=0$. It is Lebesgue integrable.

q: Compute $\int_1^e \ln(x)\,dx$ using the Lebesgue integral (equal to the Riemann integral here).
text: Compute $\int_1^e \ln(x)\,dx$ using the Lebesgue integral (equal to the Riemann integral here). Integration by parts: $\int_1^e \ln x\,dx=[x\ln x - x]_1^e=(e\cdot 1-e)-(1\cdot 0-1)=0+1=1$.

q: Let $f_n(x)=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}$. Then $f_n\to 0$ pointwise a.e. yet $\int f_n\,dm=1$ for all $n$. Which hypothesis of the Dominated Convergence Theorem fails?
text: Let $f_n(x)=n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}$. Then $f_n\to 0$ pointwise a.e. yet $\int f_n\,dm=1$ for all $n$. Which hypothesis of the Dominated Convergence Theorem fails? Any dominator must satisfy $g(x)\ge n\cdot\mathbf{1}_{(0,1/n]}(x)$ for all $n$, which forces $g(x)\ge 1/x$ near $0$. But $\int_0^1 1/x\,dm=\infty$, so no integrable dominator exists. DCT requires $|f_n|\le g$ for some $g\in L^1$, which fails here.

q: If $0\le f_n\uparrow f$ pointwise, which theorem gives $\int f_n\to\int f$?
text: If $0\le f_n\uparrow f$ pointwise, which theorem gives $\int f_n\to\int f$? MCT exactly states: for increasing nonnegative measurable $f_n$, integrals converge upward to the integral of the limit.

q: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $n$ at $n=9$.
text: Let $f_n(x)=x^n$ on $[0,1]$. Compute $\int_0^1 f_n(x)\,dx$ in terms of $n$ at $n=9$. $\int_0^1 x^n\,dx = 1/(n+1)$, so for $n=9$ the value is $1/10=0.1$.

q: Compute $i^5$ as $a+bi$.
text: Compute $i^5$ as $a+bi$. Powers of $i$ cycle every $4$: $i^5=i^{4+1}=i$.

q: Fatou's lemma states $\int\liminf_n f_n\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int f_n\,d\mu$ for $f_n\ge 0$. Which sequence shows the inequality can be strict?
text: Fatou's lemma states $\int\liminf_n f_n\,d\mu\le\liminf_n\int f_n\,d\mu$ for $f_n\ge 0$. Which sequence shows the inequality can be strict? $f_n=\mathbf{1}_{[n,n+1]}\to 0$ pointwise, so $\liminf f_n=0$ and $\int\liminf f_n=0$. But $\int f_n=1$ for all $n$, so $\liminf_n\int f_n=1>0$. Strict inequality holds because mass escapes to infinity. Look for fixed-mass bumps whose support slides off toward infinity.

q: Let $g_n(x)=\mathbf{1}_{[0,n]}(x)\cdot e^{-x}$ on $[0,\infty)$. By MCT (the sequence is increasing and nonneg), $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^\infty g_n\,dm$ equals what?
text: Let $g_n(x)=\mathbf{1}_{[0,n]}(x)\cdot e^{-x}$ on $[0,\infty)$. By MCT (the sequence is increasing and nonneg), $\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_0^\infty g_n\,dm$ equals what? $g_n\uparrow e^{-x}$ pointwise, so MCT gives $\lim\int g_n=\int_0^\infty e^{-x}\,dm=[-e^{-x}]_0^\infty=0-(-1)=1$.

q: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Monotone Convergence Theorem (for $0\le f_n\uparrow f$)?
text: Which is the correct proof-step order for the Monotone Convergence Theorem (for $0\le f_n\uparrow f$)? Standard MCT proof: monotonicity gives $\int f_n\le\int f$, so $\limsup\int f_n\le\int f$. For the reverse, fix simple $0\le s\le f$ and $c\in(0,1)$; the sets $E_n=\{f_n\ge cs\}$ increase to $X$; thus $\int f_n\ge c\int_{E_n}s\,d\mu\to c\int s\,d\mu$; let $c\to 1$ and take sup over $s$ to get $\liminf\int f_n\ge\int f$.

q: Which statement is true on a finite-measure space?
text: Which statement is true on a finite-measure space? By Hölder, on finite measure spaces one has $\|f\|_1\le \mu(X)^{1/2}\|f\|_2$, so $L^2\subset L^1$.

q: On $[0,1]$, let $f(x)=x$. Compute $\|f\|_2$.
Compute $\left|\,1-i\,\right|$.
text: Compute $\left|\,1-i\,\right|$. $|a+bi|=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, so $|1-i|=\sqrt{1^2+(-1)^2}=\sqrt2$.

q: On $[1,\infty)$ with Lebesgue measure, $f(x)=x^{-\alpha}$. For which values of $\alpha$ does $f\in L^2[1,\infty)$?
text: On $[1,\infty)$ with Lebesgue measure, $f(x)=x^{-\alpha}$. For which values of $\alpha$ does $f\in L^2[1,\infty)$? $\int_1^\infty x^{-2\alpha}\,dx$ converges iff $2\alpha>1$, i.e., $\alpha>1/2$. This is strictly weaker than $L^1$ membership (which requires $\alpha>1$), illustrating that $L^1\not\subset L^2$ on infinite-measure spaces.

q: Let $f,g\in L^2([0,1])$ with $\|f\|_2=3$ and $\|g\|_2=4$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le$ what value?
text: Let $f,g\in L^2([0,1])$ with $\|f\|_2=3$ and $\|g\|_2=4$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le$ what value? Cauchy–Schwarz: $|\langle f,g\rangle|\le\|f\|_2\|g\|_2=3\cdot 4=12$.

q: Which is the correct counterexample showing $L^1([0,1])\not\subset L^2([0,1])$?
text: Which is the correct counterexample showing $L^1([0,1])\not\subset L^2([0,1])$? $f(x)=x^{-1/2}$ satisfies $\int_0^1 x^{-1/2}\,dx=2$, so $f\in L^1$. But $\int_0^1 x^{-1}\,dx=\infty$, so $f\notin L^2$. This demonstrates that on $[0,1]$ (finite measure) the inclusion $L^2\subset L^1$ holds but the reverse fails.

title: Complex numbers
blurb: Arithmetic on $\mathbb{C} = \mathbb{R}^2$ with multiplication; polar form $z = re^{i\theta}$ and the one-point compactification to the Riemann sphere $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$ via stereographic projection.

title: Riemann sphere
blurb: Stereographic projection; ∞ as a point; circles/lines unified.

title: Möbius transformations
blurb: Linear-fractional maps, cross-ratio, automorphisms of Ĉ.

title: Amplitwist
blurb: Needham's picture: a holomorphic $f$ acts on infinitesimal vectors at $z_0$ as rotation by $\arg f'(z_0)$ and scaling by $|f'(z_0)|$; the Jacobian is a similarity matrix.

title: Cauchy–Riemann equations
blurb: A complex-differentiable $f = u + iv$ must have Jacobian commuting with multiplication by $i$; equivalently $u_x = v_y$, $u_y = -v_x$, i.e. $\bar\partial f = 0$.

title: Holomorphic functions
blurb: Complex-differentiable on an open set; infinitely more rigid than real.

title: Domain coloring
blurb: Visualize $f\colon\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}$ with hue $=\arg f(z)$ and lightness encoding $|f(z)|$; zeros and poles appear as rainbow winds counting multiplicity.

title: Contour integrals
blurb: Integrate $f\,dz$ along a piecewise smooth $\gamma$; the real 1-forms $u\,dx - v\,dy$ and $v\,dx + u\,dy$ are closed exactly when $f$ is holomorphic.

title: Cauchy's theorem
blurb: ∫_γ f dz = 0 on simply connected domains.

title: Cauchy integral formula
blurb: $f(a) = \tfrac{1}{2\pi i}\oint f(z)/(z-a)\,dz$: boundary values determine interior values on a closed disk, and differentiation under the integral makes holomorphic functions $C^\infty$.

title: Analyticity = holomorphy
blurb: Every holomorphic function is locally a convergent power series.

title: Liouville's theorem
blurb: Bounded entire ⇒ constant.

title: Fundamental theorem of algebra
blurb: Every non-constant polynomial over $\mathbb{C}$ has a root; Liouville's argument applied to $1/p(z)$ makes this a corollary of complex boundedness.

title: Maximum modulus principle
blurb: Non-constant holomorphic f has no interior max of |f|.

title: Schwarz lemma
blurb: For holomorphic $f\colon\mathbb{D}\to\mathbb{D}$ with $f(0)=0$: $|f(z)|\le|z|$ and $|f'(0)|\le 1$; equality forces $f(z)=e^{i\theta}z$, a rigidity extracted from the maximum modulus principle.

title: Laurent series
blurb: Expansions on annuli; principal part captures singular behavior.

title: Classification of singularities
blurb: Removable, pole of order n, essential (Casorati–Weierstrass).

title: Residue theorem
blurb: ∮ f dz = 2πi · Σ Res(f; z_k). Computes real integrals, sums series.

title: Argument principle
blurb: (1/2πi) ∮ f′/f = Z − P; winding of f(γ) counts zeros minus poles.

title: Rouché's theorem
blurb: |g| < |f| on γ ⇒ f and f+g have the same number of zeros inside.

title: Conformal maps
blurb: Angle-preserving, orientation-preserving; locally holomorphic with f′ ≠ 0.

title: Disk automorphisms
blurb: $\mathrm{Aut}(\mathbb{D})$ consists of the Blaschke factors $e^{i\theta}(z-a)/(1-\bar a z)$; this is the isometry group $\mathrm{PSU}(1,1)$ of the hyperbolic Poincaré metric on the disk.

title: Normal families (Montel)
blurb: Montel: a locally bounded family of holomorphic functions is normal (every sequence has a locally uniformly convergent subsequence); compactness that powers the Riemann mapping theorem.

title: Riemann mapping theorem
blurb: Any simply connected proper open U ⊂ C is conformally equivalent to D.

title: Harmonic functions
blurb: If $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic then $u$ and $v$ satisfy Laplace's equation $\Delta u = 0$; on simply connected domains every harmonic $u$ has a harmonic conjugate recovering a holomorphic lift.

title: Analytic continuation
blurb: The identity theorem lets a holomorphic germ extend uniquely along paths; loops around branch points produce monodromy, and $\log z$ or $\sqrt z$ live naturally on a Riemann surface rather than $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$.

q: Compute $|3 + 4i|$.
Multiply $(1+i)(2-i)$.
What is $\arg(-1 - i)$ in $(-\pi, \pi]$?
text: What is $\arg(-1 - i)$ in $(-\pi, \pi]$? $-1-i$ lies in the third quadrant. Its modulus is $\sqrt{2}$ and its angle from the positive real axis (measured in $(-\pi,\pi]$) is $-3\pi/4$.

q: On the complex plane below, place a marker at the primitive sixth root of unity $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3}$. Real axis runs left-right; imaginary axis runs up (note the SVG $y$-axis has been flipped so positive imaginary is up). Unit circle is shown.
text: On the complex plane below, place a marker at the primitive sixth root of unity $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3}$. Real axis runs left-right; imaginary axis runs up (note the SVG $y$-axis has been flipped so positive imaginary is up). Unit circle is shown. The primitive sixth root of unity is $\zeta_6 = e^{i\pi/3} = \cos(\pi/3) + i\sin(\pi/3) = 1/2 + i\sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.5 + 0.866 i$. In the SVG viewBox the $y$-axis points down, so the target coordinate is $(0.5, -\sqrt{3}/2)$. $\zeta_6 = \cos(\pi/3) + i\sin(\pi/3) = 1/2 + i\sqrt{3}/2$.

q: Compute the sum of all 4th roots of $-16$ as a single complex number $a + bi$.
text: Compute the sum of all 4th roots of $-16$ as a single complex number $a + bi$. The 4th roots of $-16 = 16 e^{i\pi}$ are $2e^{i(\pi + 2\pi k)/4}$ for $k=0,1,2,3$. Their sum equals $2(e^{i\pi/4}+e^{3i\pi/4}+e^{5i\pi/4}+e^{7i\pi/4}) = 0$, since the roots of any $z^n = c$ sum to $0$ when $n \ge 2$. The roots lie at the vertices of a regular polygon centered at the origin.

q: Which property of $|\cdot|$ on $\mathbb{C}$ fails for the function $f(z) = \operatorname{Re}(z)$?
text: Which property of $|\cdot|$ on $\mathbb{C}$ fails for the function $f(z) = \operatorname{Re}(z)$? $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda z) = \operatorname{Re}(\lambda)\operatorname{Re}(z) - \operatorname{Im}(\lambda)\operatorname{Im}(z)$, which does not equal $|\lambda|\operatorname{Re}(z)$ in general (e.g.~$\lambda = i$, $z = 1$ gives $\operatorname{Re}(i \cdot 1) = 0 \neq 1 \cdot 1$). Also positive-definiteness fails ($f(i) = 0$ but $i \neq 0$), but homogeneity is the cleanest single counterexample structural failure of a norm.

q: How many fixed points does a non-identity Möbius transformation have on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$?
text: How many fixed points does a non-identity Möbius transformation have on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$? Solving $T(z) = z$ gives a quadratic $cz^2 + (d-a)z - b = 0$ on $\mathbb{C}$, with $\infty$ a fixed point exactly when $c = 0$. Always exactly two roots in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$ counted with multiplicity.

q: Let $T(z) = \dfrac{z-1}{z+1}$. Compute $T(0)$.
How many distinct points must you specify the image of in order to uniquely determine a Möbius transformation?
text: How many distinct points must you specify the image of in order to uniquely determine a Möbius transformation? Möbius maps are 3-transitive on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$: any triple of distinct points can be sent to any other triple by a unique transformation.

q: The cross-ratio $(z_1, z_2; z_3, z_4)$ is invariant under Möbius transformations. If $(0, 1; z, \infty) = 2$, what is $z$?
text: The cross-ratio $(z_1, z_2; z_3, z_4)$ is invariant under Möbius transformations. If $(0, 1; z, \infty) = 2$, what is $z$? The cross-ratio $(z_1,z_2;z_3,z_4)=\frac{(z_1-z_3)(z_2-z_4)}{(z_1-z_4)(z_2-z_3)}$. With $z_4=\infty$ this simplifies to $\frac{z_1-z_3}{z_2-z_3}=\frac{0-z}{1-z}$. Setting equal to $2$: $-z = 2(1-z) = 2-2z$, so $z = 2 - 2z + z \Rightarrow z = 1/2$.

q: A Möbius transformation $T$ has exactly one fixed point in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. Which type is $T$?
text: A Möbius transformation $T$ has exactly one fixed point in $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. Which type is $T$? A non-identity Möbius transformation has two fixed points counted with multiplicity. Exactly one (a double fixed point) occurs precisely when the transformation is parabolic, i.e., conjugate to $z \mapsto z + 1$, and algebraically characterized by $\mathrm{tr}^2 = 4$.

q: Match each Möbius transformation with its geometric role on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$.
text: Match each Möbius transformation with its geometric role on $\hat{\mathbb{C}}$. $T(z)=z+1$ is translation; $T(z)=1/z$ is the classical inversion $z\mapsto 1/z$ (the map whose fixed points are $\pm 1$ and which swaps $0$ and $\infty$); $T(z)=rz$ is a dilation; $T(z)=e^{i\theta}z$ is a rotation by $\theta$.

q: For $f(z) = z^2$ at $z_0 = 1 + i$, the amplitwist acts on infinitesimal vectors by:
text: For $f(z) = z^2$ at $z_0 = 1 + i$, the amplitwist acts on infinitesimal vectors by: $f'(z) = 2z$ so $f'(1+i) = 2 + 2i$. Then $|f'(z_0)| = 2\sqrt{2}$ and $\arg f'(z_0) = \pi/4$.

q: If $f$ is holomorphic at $z_0$ and $f'(z_0) = 0$, is $f$ conformal (angle-preserving) at $z_0$?
text: If $f$ is holomorphic at $z_0$ and $f'(z_0) = 0$, is $f$ conformal (angle-preserving) at $z_0$? Where $f'(z_0)=0$, $f$ is not a similarity at first order; if $f$ vanishes to order $n$ at $z_0$, angles between curves through $z_0$ get multiplied by $n$.

q: For $f(z) = z^3$ at $z_0 = i$, what is $|f'(z_0)|$?
For $f(z) = z^2 + iz$ near $z_0 = 1 - i$, compute the rotation angle (in radians) that the amplitwist applies to infinitesimal tangent vectors. Give a value in $(-\pi, \pi]$.
text: For $f(z) = z^2 + iz$ near $z_0 = 1 - i$, compute the rotation angle (in radians) that the amplitwist applies to infinitesimal tangent vectors. Give a value in $(-\pi, \pi]$. $f'(z) = 2z + i$, so $f'(1-i) = 2(1-i)+i = 2 - 2i + i = 2 - i$. The rotation angle is $\arg(2-i) = -\arctan(1/2) \approx -0.4636$ radians.

q: Let $f(z) = z^n$ for $n \ge 2$. At $z_0 = 0$ the map $f$ is not conformal. By what factor are angles between smooth curves through $0$ multiplied by $f$?
text: Let $f(z) = z^n$ for $n \ge 2$. At $z_0 = 0$ the map $f$ is not conformal. By what factor are angles between smooth curves through $0$ multiplied by $f$? At a zero of $f'$ of order $n-1$ (i.e., $f(z) = z^n$ has $f'(0) = 0$ to order $n-1$), the map behaves like $z \mapsto z^n$ locally, which multiplies angles by $n$. Two curves meeting at angle $\alpha$ get mapped to curves meeting at angle $n\alpha$.

q: Which of these functions is holomorphic on all of $\mathbb{C}$?
text: Which of these functions is holomorphic on all of $\mathbb{C}$? Anything involving $\bar z$ explicitly fails the Cauchy–Riemann equations. Polynomials in $z$ alone are entire.

q: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$. Find the unique harmonic conjugate $v$ with $v(0,0) = 0$ such that $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic, and compute $v(1, 2)$.
text: Let $u(x,y) = x^2 - y^2$. Find the unique harmonic conjugate $v$ with $v(0,0) = 0$ such that $f = u + iv$ is holomorphic, and compute $v(1, 2)$. From $v_y = u_x = 2x$ we get $v = 2xy + g(x)$; then $-v_x = u_y = -2y$ forces $g'(x) = 0$, so $v = 2xy$. Thus $f = z^2$ and $v(1,2) = 4$.

q: Which is the correct form of the Cauchy–Riemann equations for $f = u + iv$?
text: Which is the correct form of the Cauchy–Riemann equations for $f = u + iv$? Equivalently: $\partial_{\bar z} f = 0$, where $\partial_{\bar z} = \tfrac12(\partial_x + i\partial_y)$.

q: The function $f(z) = |z|^2$ satisfies the Cauchy–Riemann equations at exactly one point. Which point?
text: The function $f(z) = |z|^2$ satisfies the Cauchy–Riemann equations at exactly one point. Which point? Write $f = x^2 + y^2 + 0i$, so $u = x^2+y^2$, $v = 0$. Then $u_x = 2x$, $v_y = 0$, $u_y = 2y$, $v_x = 0$. The C–R equations $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$ become $2x = 0$ and $2y = 0$, satisfied only at $z = 0$. But $f$ is not holomorphic there since the partial derivatives are not continuous in a neighborhood consistent with complex differentiability at any nonzero radius.

q: Let $u(x,y) = e^x \cos y$. The harmonic conjugate with $v(0,0) = 0$ is $v(x,y) = e^x \sin y$. Compute $\operatorname{Im}(f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2))$ where $f = u + iv$.
text: Let $u(x,y) = e^x \cos y$. The harmonic conjugate with $v(0,0) = 0$ is $v(x,y) = e^x \sin y$. Compute $\operatorname{Im}(f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2))$ where $f = u + iv$. $f(z) = e^z$ (since $u = \operatorname{Re}(e^z)$, $v = \operatorname{Im}(e^z)$). Then $f(\ln 2 + i\pi/2) = e^{\ln 2}e^{i\pi/2} = 2i$, so $\operatorname{Im}(f) = 2$.

q: Compute $\operatorname{Res}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{z^2+1};\, i\bigr)$.
text: Compute $\operatorname{Res}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{z^2+1};\, i\bigr)$. $1/(z^2+1) = 1/((z-i)(z+i))$, simple pole at $i$ with residue $1/(2i) = -i/2$.

q: Compute $\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{dz}{z^2+1}$.
text: Compute $\displaystyle\oint_{|z|=2}\frac{dz}{z^2+1}$. Both poles $\pm i$ are inside the contour. $\operatorname{Res}(\cdot;i) = -i/2$, $\operatorname{Res}(\cdot;-i) = i/2$, sum $0$. So the integral is $2\pi i \cdot 0 = 0$.

q: For $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2(z-1)}$, compute $\operatorname{Res}(f; 0)$.
text: For $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z^2(z-1)}$, compute $\operatorname{Res}(f; 0)$. Pole of order 2 at $0$. $\operatorname{Res}(f;0) = \lim_{z\to 0}\frac{d}{dz}\!\left[z^2 f(z)\right] = \frac{d}{dz}\!\left[\frac{1}{z-1}\right]_{z=0} = -\frac{1}{(0-1)^2} = -1$.

q: Use the residue theorem to evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \frac{dx}{1+x^4}$. Enter the exact value as a decimal to three places.
text: Use the residue theorem to evaluate $\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} \frac{dx}{1+x^4}$. Enter the exact value as a decimal to three places. The integrand $1/(1+z^4)$ has poles at $z_k = e^{i\pi(2k+1)/4}$, $k=0,1,2,3$. For the upper half-plane contour, the poles inside are $z_0 = e^{i\pi/4}$ and $z_1 = e^{3i\pi/4}$. Computing: $\operatorname{Res}(f;z_k) = 1/(4z_k^3) = -z_k/4$. The two residues sum to $-(e^{i\pi/4}+e^{3i\pi/4})/4 = -(i\sqrt{2}/2) \cdot (1/2)$... The standard result is $\int_0^\infty dx/(1+x^4) = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.1107$. Close the contour in the upper half-plane and use evenness to halve the real-line integral.

q: The function $f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z^3}$ has a singularity at $z=0$. What kind is it, and what is $\operatorname{Res}(f;0)$?
text: The function $f(z) = \dfrac{\sin z}{z^3}$ has a singularity at $z=0$. What kind is it, and what is $\operatorname{Res}(f;0)$? Laurent expand: $\sin z/z^3 = (z - z^3/6 + z^5/120 - \cdots)/z^3 = z^{-2} - 1/6 + z^2/120 - \cdots$. The principal part has lowest term $z^{-2}$, so this is a pole of order 2. The residue is the coefficient of $z^{-1}$, which is $0$ (no $z^{-1}$ term appears in the expansion). Write the Taylor series of $\sin z$ and divide term by term.

q: Arrange the steps of a standard residue-theorem contour-integral proof into the order they appear in the argument.
text: Arrange the steps of a standard residue-theorem contour-integral proof into the order they appear in the argument. The standard playbook proceeds exactly in this order: (1) locate poles of the integrand and decide which are enclosed; (2) close the real axis with a large semicircle so the integral becomes a loop; (3) use Jordan's lemma or a $1/R^k$ decay estimate to kill the arc contribution as $R\to\infty$; (4) apply the residue theorem to the closed contour; (5) since the arc vanished, the loop integral equals the real-line integral, giving the answer. Think of the workflow: classify singularities first, build the contour, estimate the arc, invoke the theorem, then extract the real answer.

title: Banach and Hilbert spaces
blurb: Infinite-dimensional normed spaces provide the analytic home for operators and PDEs.

title: Bounded operators and adjoints
blurb: Linear operators on Hilbert spaces admit operator norms, adjoints, and projection geometry.

title: Spectral theorem viewpoint
blurb: Self-adjoint and normal operators are analyzed through spectral decompositions.

title: Riesz representation theorem
blurb: Every bounded linear functional on a Hilbert space is inner product with a unique vector; on Lᵖ it is integration against an Lᵩ density.

title: Weak and weak-* topologies
blurb: Coarsen the topology so dual pairings become continuous; Banach–Alaoglu then makes the unit ball of the dual weak-* compact.

q: Which condition distinguishes a Banach space from an arbitrary normed space?
text: Which condition distinguishes a Banach space from an arbitrary normed space? A Banach space is a normed vector space in which every Cauchy sequence converges. Inner products give Hilbert spaces; compact unit balls characterize finite-dimensional normed spaces.

q: A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on a real vector space arises from an inner product if and only if it satisfies:
text: A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on a real vector space arises from an inner product if and only if it satisfies: The Jordan–von Neumann theorem: a norm comes from an inner product iff it obeys the parallelogram law. The inner product is then recovered by polarization.

q: In $\ell^2$, compute $\|x\|_2$ for $x=(1,2,2,0,0,\ldots)$.
The space $C[0,1]$ with the $L^2$ norm is NOT a Banach space. Which is the correct reason, and which is its completion?
text: The space $C[0,1]$ with the $L^2$ norm is NOT a Banach space. Which is the correct reason, and which is its completion? The functions $f_n(x)=x^n$ form a Cauchy sequence in $C[0,1]$ under $\|\cdot\|_{L^2}$, but their $L^2$ limit is the indicator $\mathbf{1}_{{1}}$, which is not continuous. Thus $C[0,1]$ is not closed under $L^2$ limits; its metric completion is $L^2[0,1]$.

q: Which is the correct order of steps for proving $\ell^2$ is a Hilbert space via the Riesz–Fischer approach?
