text: Which of the following guarantees that an affine open cover of a scheme is Leray for any quasi-coherent sheaf? Serre proved that affine schemes have no higher quasi-coherent cohomology, so every finite intersection of affine opens (also affine when the scheme is separated) is $\mathcal{F}$-acyclic. Hence an affine open cover is Leray.

q: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$?
text: The standard affine cover of $\mathbb{P}^2$ has three charts $U_0,U_1,U_2$. How many terms does the resulting Čech complex $\check C^0 \to \check C^1 \to \check C^2$ have in $\check C^1$? $\check C^1$ indexes strictly-increasing pairs $(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)$ from $\{0,1,2\}$, so its rank is $\binom{3}{2} = 3$. (Triple intersections contribute $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term in $\check C^2$.)

q: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$?
text: Let $X=\mathbb{P}^1_k$ with affine cover $U_0=\{x_0\ne 0\}$, $U_1=\{x_1\ne 0\}$, and $\mathcal{F}=\mathcal{O}(-3)$. Which statement correctly explains why this cover is Leray for $\mathcal{O}(-3)$? Each chart and their intersection are affine schemes. Serre's vanishing theorem for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes ensures $H^i>0$ vanishes on each piece, making the cover Leray and the Čech computation exact. On affine opens, quasi-coherent cohomology vanishes in positive degree.

q: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have?
text: Using the Leray cover $\{U_0,U_1\}$ of $\mathbb{P}^2$ (standard affine cover by three charts), the Čech complex for a quasi-coherent sheaf has terms in degrees $0,1,2$. How many terms does $\check{C}^2$ have? $\check{C}^2$ indexes strictly-increasing triples from $\{0,1,2\}$: only $(0,1,2)$, giving $\binom{3}{3}=1$ term. Check that triple intersections are affine.

q: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid?
text: If $\mathcal{U}$ is a Leray cover for $\mathcal{F}$ but NOT for $\mathcal{G}$, and there is a map $\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{G}$, which conclusion is valid? The Leray isomorphism $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F})\cong H^p(X,\mathcal{F})$ applies sheaf-by-sheaf; $\mathcal{F}$ satisfies the hypothesis but $\mathcal{G}$ does not, so the Čech-to-derived comparison holds for $\mathcal{F}$ and may fail for $\mathcal{G}$. Spectral sequence mismatch breaks Leray calculation for $\mathcal{G}$.

q: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction?
text: A short exact sequence of sheaves $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$ produces a long exact sequence whose connecting homomorphism $\delta$ goes in which direction? The connecting map raises degree by one and jumps from the quotient sheaf back to the subsheaf: $\delta\colon H^p(\mathcal{F}'') \to H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$. In Čech language it lifts a local section of $\mathcal{F}''$ to one of $\mathcal{F}$; the failure of the lifts to glue is a class in $H^{p+1}(\mathcal{F}')$.

q: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to?
text: In the exponential sequence $0 \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}} \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}^\times \to 0$ on $\mathbb{C}^\times$, what does $\delta\colon H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^1(\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ send the global section $z$ to? On $\mathbb{C}^\times$ the global section $z$ has no single-valued logarithm, so its image under $\delta$ is non-trivial. Concretely the class is $1\in H^1(\mathbb{C}^\times,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}) = \mathbb{Z}$, the winding-number generator.

q: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$.
text: From $0\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}\to\mathcal{O}_p\to 0$ (skyscraper at a point) and the vanishing $H^i(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}^1}(-1))=0$ for all $i$, deduce $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}_p)$. The sandwiching $H^i(\mathcal{O}(-1)) = 0$ forces $H^0(\mathcal{O}) \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\mathcal{O}_p)$. Since $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}) = 1$, we get $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}_p) = 1$ — the stalk at $p$.

q: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant?
text: From the exponential sequence $0\to\underline{\mathbb{Z}}\to\mathcal{O}_X\to\mathcal{O}_X^\times\to 0$ on a compact Riemann surface $X$, the connecting map $\delta\colon H^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)\to H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})$ sends a line bundle $L$ to which invariant? The connecting homomorphism $\delta$ in the long exact sequence of the exponential sequence sends a holomorphic line bundle (class in $H^1(\mathcal{O}^\times)=\mathrm{Pic}(X)$) to its first Chern class in $H^2(X,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, which equals the degree of the line bundle. Extract $H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}) \to H^1(X, \mathcal{O}^\times) \to H^2(X, \mathbb{Z})$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$, apply the long exact sequence of $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}^{\oplus 2}\to\mathcal{O}(1)\to 0$ (Euler sequence twisted). Given $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O})=1$, $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(1))$. From the long exact sequence: $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})^2\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since the flanking terms vanish, the middle map is an isomorphism followed by a surjection: $\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(1))=2\cdot 1=2$. Apply $H^0$ and use the Euler exact sequence.

q: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$?
text: Which is the correct order of steps to construct the connecting homomorphism $\delta\colon H^p(X,\mathcal{F}'')\to H^{p+1}(X,\mathcal{F}')$ from a SES $0\to\mathcal{F}'\to\mathcal{F}\to\mathcal{F}''\to 0$? The standard construction: embed each sheaf in an injective sheaf, extend to a SES of injective resolutions (which splits in each degree by injectivity of $I'^p$), apply $\Gamma$, and apply the snake lemma to the resulting SES of cochain complexes. The resulting boundary map on cohomology is $\delta$. Zig-zag in the snake lemma gives $\delta$.

q: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$?
text: Which property of the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ is measured by the derived functors $R^i\Gamma$? $\Gamma(X,-)$ is left exact but not right exact; $R^i\Gamma$ measures exactly the right-exactness defect via a long exact sequence.

q: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic?
text: Which class of sheaves is NOT automatically $\Gamma$-acyclic? Injective, flabby, and soft (on paracompact Hausdorff) sheaves are all $\Gamma$-acyclic. A locally constant sheaf like $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has nonzero $H^1$, so it is not acyclic in general.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1_k$ the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(-5)$ has $h^0=0$. Using $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(n))=\max(-n-1,0)$, compute $\dim_k H^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-5))$. $\max(-(-5)-1,0)=\max(4,0)=4$.

q: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why?
text: Which of these sheaves on $S^1$ is NOT $\Gamma$-acyclic, and why? The constant sheaf $\underline{\mathbb{Z}}$ on $S^1$ has $H^1(S^1,\underline{\mathbb{Z}})\cong\mathbb{Z}$, witnessing the winding-number class. Flabby and injective sheaves are acyclic by construction; skyscraper sheaves at a point have $H^i=0$ for $i>0$ since points are acyclic. Constant sheaves on $S^1$ have nontrivial $H^1$ detecting the loop.

q: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$.
text: For $\mathbb{P}^2_k$, the formula $\dim H^1(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O}(n))=0$ for all $n$. Using this and the exact sequence $0\to\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}_H\to 0$ (restriction to a hyperplane $H\cong\mathbb{P}^1$), compute $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})$ given $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. The long exact sequence gives $0\to H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}_H)\to H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))\to\cdots$. Since $H^0(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$ and $H^1(\mathcal{O}(-1))=0$, we get $H^0(\mathbb{P}^2,\mathcal{O})\cong H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O})=1$. Combine short exact sequence with vanishing of $H^1(\mathcal{O}(n))$.

q: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)?
text: Why does the global-sections functor $\Gamma(X,-)$ fail to be right exact on $\mathbf{Ab}(X)$ (abelian sheaves on $X$)? A morphism of sheaves is surjective iff it is surjective on stalks. Global sections need not be surjective: the exponential $\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{O}^\times$ is locally surjective (every holomorphic function has local logarithms), but $z\in H^0(\mathbb{C}^\times,\mathcal{O}^\times)$ has no global logarithm, so $H^0(\mathcal{O})\to H^0(\mathcal{O}^\times)$ is not surjective. Sections functor is only left exact without injective resolutions.

q: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group?
text: The Picard group of a ringed space $(X,\mathcal{O}_X)$ is canonically isomorphic to which Čech cohomology group? Line bundles are classified by their transition cocycles in $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ modulo coboundaries: $\mathrm{Pic}(X)\cong\check{H}^1(X,\mathcal{O}_X^\times)$.

q: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension.
text: On $\mathbb{P}^1$ with $\omega_{\mathbb{P}^1}=\mathcal{O}(-2)$, Serre duality gives $\dim H^1(\mathcal{O}(-7))=\dim H^0(\mathcal{O}(7-2))$. Compute this common dimension. $\dim H^0(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(5))=5+1=6$, and $\max(-(-7)-1,0)=6$, so the duality check holds.

q: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$.
text: Use Riemann–Roch on a smooth projective curve of genus $g=4$ together with Serre duality to compute $\dim H^0(C,L)-\dim H^0(C,\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$ for a line bundle $L$ of degree $d=10$. Riemann–Roch: $h^0(L)-h^1(L)=d-g+1=10-4+1=7$, and Serre duality identifies $h^1(L)=h^0(\omega_C\otimes L^{-1})$.

q: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct?
text: On an elliptic curve $E$ (genus $1$, $\omega_E\cong\mathcal{O}_E$), Serre duality gives $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. For a degree-$3$ line bundle $L$, which statement is correct? Serre duality: $H^1(E,L)\cong H^0(E,\omega_E\otimes L^{-1})^\vee = H^0(E,L^{-1})^\vee$. Since $L^{-1}$ has degree $-3<0$, $H^0(E,L^{-1})=0$, so $\dim H^1(E,L)=0$. Then Riemann–Roch gives $\dim H^0(E,L)=\deg L -g+1=3$. Serre duality gives $H^1 \cong H^0$-dual for self-dual canonical.

q: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality.
text: For the projective line $\mathbb{P}^1$ and the line bundle $\mathcal{O}(n)$, Serre duality says $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$. Compute $h^1(\mathbb{P}^1,\mathcal{O}(-4))$ using this duality. $h^1(\mathcal{O}(-4))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(-(-4)-2))=h^0(\mathcal{O}(2))=2+1=3$. Compute $h^1(\mathcal{O}(n)) = h^0(\mathcal{O}(-n-2))$.

q: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$?
text: The Picard group $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathbb{P}^1_k)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is generated by $\mathcal{O}(1)$. The exponential sequence on a smooth projective curve $C$ over $\mathbb{C}$ gives an exact sequence involving $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$. Which statement correctly identifies $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)$? From the exponential sequence, the degree-zero line bundles form $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)=\ker(c_1)$. The long exact sequence gives $H^1(C,\mathcal{O})\to H^1(C,\mathcal{O}^\times)\xrightarrow{c_1} H^2(C,\mathbb{Z})$, and $\mathrm{Pic}^0(C)\cong H^1(C,\mathcal{O})/\mathrm{image}(H^1(C,\underline{\mathbb{Z}}))$, a $g$-dimensional complex torus — the Jacobian of $C$. Pic of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is detected by $H^1$ of structure sheaf cocycles.

title: Groupoids, fibered categories, descent
blurb: Stacks encode objects with automorphisms while preserving gluing across covers.

title: Classifying stacks BG
blurb: BG packages principal G-bundles functorially and acts as a universal quotient object.

title: Quotient stack [X/G]
blurb: The stack [X/G] records a G-action on X while remembering stabilisers; BG is the case X = point.

title: Deligne–Mumford vs. Artin stacks
blurb: Dropping 'étale atlas' to 'smooth atlas' enlarges DM stacks to Artin stacks, accommodating positive-dimensional automorphisms.

title: Deligne–Mumford stacks and coarse spaces
blurb: DM stacks model orbifold-like moduli with étale atlases and coarse moduli maps.

q: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable?
text: Why must a moduli functor $\mathcal{M}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ for elliptic curves fail to be representable? Every elliptic curve has the automorphism $[-1]$; twisting a family by this automorphism along a cover produces a non-isomorphic family with the same classifying map to any putative fine moduli scheme. Passing to groupoid-valued functors is precisely what records this data.

q: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$:
text: A fibered category $\mathcal{X}\to\mathrm{Sch}$ is a stack (in the étale topology) iff for every cover $\{U_i\to U\}$: The stack axiom has two layers: isomorphism sheaves must glue locally, and objects given by compatible local data (a descent datum satisfying the cocycle on triple overlaps) must glue to a global object unique up to unique isomorphism.

q: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have?
text: Let $G$ be a groupoid with three objects, each having automorphism group $\mathbb{Z}/2$, all connected by isomorphisms. How many isomorphism classes does $\pi_0(G)$ have? All three objects are connected by isomorphisms, so they form a single isomorphism class: $\pi_0(G)$ has one element. The three $\mathbb{Z}/2$ automorphism groups are what is lost when one collapses to $\pi_0$.

q: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate?
text: A fibered category $p:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{C}$ satisfies descent for a cover $\{U_i\to U\}$ but the isomorphism-sheaf condition fails for some pair of objects. Which axiom of a stack does this violate? A stack requires two conditions: (1) $\mathrm{Isom}(x,y)$ is a sheaf (morphism sheaf axiom) and (2) descent data for objects glue. Failure of the Isom sheaf condition violates the first axiom while leaving the second intact. Descent is effective only if isomorphism sheaves are actually sheaves.

q: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology?
text: Which of these is a counterexample showing that the naive moduli set-functor $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}^{\mathrm{naive}}:\mathrm{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathrm{Set}$ (assigning iso-classes of elliptic curves) is NOT a sheaf in the étale topology? Twisting a constant family by a non-trivial $\mu_2$-torsor (via the $[-1]$ automorphism) over an étale cover produces an isotrivial but non-trivial family. The cocycle data exist, but they glue to different isomorphism-class representatives depending on the chosen descent isomorphisms — violating sheafness. Automorphisms collapse objects with $j$-invariant $0$ or $1728$.

q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain?
text: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the groupoid with objects $\{A,B,C\}$, morphisms forming a single isomorphism class, and $\mathrm{Aut}(A)=\{e\}$. After passing to $\pi_0$, how many objects remain? All three objects are isomorphic, so they represent a single isomorphism class. $|\pi_0(\mathcal{C})|=1$. The trivial automorphism groups are irrelevant to $\pi_0$. Automorphism groups give isotropy, not multiplicity.

q: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\mathrm{Spec}\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme?
text: The classifying stack $BG$ is defined by $BG(R)=$ groupoid of $G$-torsors on $\mathrm{Spec}\,R$. Why can $BG$ not be a scheme? A scheme's functor of points lands in sets, so its geometric points have trivial automorphism groups. $BG$ has automorphism group $G$ at its trivial torsor, which records pure automorphism data and prevents representability by any scheme.

q: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.)
text: How many isomorphism classes of $\mathbb{Z}/3$-torsors are there on $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q$ for a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$? (Equivalently, $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{F}_q,\mathbb{Z}/3)|$, using that the absolute Galois group of $\mathbb{F}_q$ is $\widehat{\mathbb{Z}}$ and $\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)=\mathbb{Z}/3$.) $\mathrm{Hom}_{\text{cont}}(\widehat{\mathbb{Z}},\mathbb{Z}/3)\cong \mathbb{Z}/3$ has 3 elements, one for each element of $\mathbb{Z}/3$. Hence $|\pi_0(B(\mathbb{Z}/3)(\mathbb{F}_q))|=3$.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$).
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/2$ over $\mathbb{R}$: $\mathrm{Gal}(\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$, so $|\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)|=?$ (this counts iso classes of $\mathbb{Z}/2$-torsors over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$). $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\{0,\mathrm{id}\}$ has 2 elements: the trivial torsor and the non-trivial one corresponding to $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{C}\to\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{R}$.

q: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack?
text: For any algebraic group $G$, the inertia stack $I(BG)=BG\times_{BG\times BG}BG$ is equivalent to which stack? The inertia stack of $BG$ parametrises pairs (torsor, automorphism), which is the same data as a $G$-torsor together with a section of the associated group scheme — i.e., an element of $G$ up to conjugation. This is exactly $[G/G]$ for the conjugation action. The inertia stack parameterizes objects with an automorphism.

q: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$?
text: For $G=\mathbb{Z}/5$ over an algebraically closed field $k$ with $\mathrm{char}(k)\ne 5$, how many isomorphism classes of $G$-torsors are there over $\mathrm{Spec}\,k$? Over an algebraically closed field, $H^1_{\text{ét}}(\mathrm{Spec}\,k,G)=0$ for any finite group $G$ of order coprime to the characteristic. So there is exactly $1$ isomorphism class: the trivial torsor. Use $|H^1(\mathrm{Spec}\,k, \mathbb{Z}/5)| = 1$.

q: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is:
text: The correct chain of implications among stack conditions is: Every scheme is an algebraic space (trivial groupoid structure); every algebraic space is a DM stack (étale atlas, trivial automorphisms); every DM stack is an Artin stack (étale implies smooth). The reverse implications all fail: $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM; $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is DM but not an algebraic space. Separated implies quasi-separated; Artin is weaker than Deligne-Mumford.

q: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is:
text: A $T$-point of the quotient stack $[X/G]$ is: By definition $[X/G](T)$ is the groupoid of pairs $(P\to T,\ \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. Setting $X=\mathrm{pt}$ recovers $BG$; setting $G=\{e\}$ recovers $X$ itself.

q: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$?
text: When does $[X/G]$ coincide (as stacks) with the quotient scheme $X/G$? If the action is free, every stabiliser is trivial and no automorphism data needs to be remembered, so $[X/G]$ is the ordinary quotient $X/G$. Non-free actions create non-trivial stabilisers and force stacky structure.

q: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$?
text: For $G=\mu_3$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x = \zeta x$, the stabiliser at $x=0$ is all of $\mu_3$ and is trivial elsewhere. What is the order of the automorphism group of the origin in $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$? $\mathrm{Stab}_{\mu_3}(0)=\mu_3$ has order $3$. Geometrically this is the stacky enhancement at the fixed point; away from $0$ the quotient is an ordinary scheme.

q: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object?
text: Let $G=\mathbb{G}_m$ act on $X=\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ by $\lambda\cdot(x,y)=(\lambda x,\lambda y)$. The quotient stack $[X/G]$ is equivalent to which classical geometric object? $\mathbb{G}_m$ acts freely on $\mathbb{A}^2\setminus\{0\}$ (no non-trivial stabilisers), so $[X/G]$ is an algebraic space and in fact coincides with the ordinary quotient scheme $\mathbb{P}^1$. The scheme-theoretic quotient is $\mathbb{P}^1$; stacky data is trivial here.

q: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$?
text: A $T$-point of $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$ is a $G$-torsor on $T$. What is the correct chain of equivalences proving $[\mathrm{pt}/G]=BG$? A $T$-point of $[X/G]$ is $(P\to T, \phi:P\to X)$ with $P$ a $G$-torsor and $\phi$ equivariant. For $X=\mathrm{pt}$, $\phi$ is forced to be the unique map $P\to\mathrm{pt}$, so the data reduces to just the $G$-torsor $P\to T$. Hence $[\mathrm{pt}/G](T)\simeq BG(T)$ for all $T$. Unwind definitions: principal bundle over $T$ is a $G$-torsor.

q: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order?
text: For $G=\mu_2$ acting on $\mathbb{A}^1$ by $\zeta\cdot x=-x$, the coarse moduli space of $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_2]$ is $\mathbb{A}^1$ (via $x\mapsto x^2$). At the stacky point $x=0$, the automorphism group has order? The stabiliser of $0$ under the $\mu_2$ action $x\mapsto -x$ is all of $\mu_2$, which has order $2$. Away from $0$ the stabiliser is trivial. Inertia at the origin is $\mu_2$; elsewhere trivial.

q: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack?
text: Which condition distinguishes a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin stack? Artin requires a smooth atlas and a representable diagonal; DM additionally requires the atlas to be étale, equivalently the diagonal to be unramified. This forces automorphism schemes to be $0$-dimensional (finite and unramified).

q: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which of the following stacks is Artin but NOT Deligne-Mumford? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$, which is $1$-dimensional — so it is Artin but not DM. $B\mu_n$ and $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ have finite automorphism groups, and $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ has finite automorphisms away from char $2,3$.

q: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$?
text: What is the dimension of the automorphism group at the generic geometric point of $B\mathbb{G}_m$? $B\mathbb{G}_m$ has automorphism group $\mathbb{G}_m$ (the multiplicative group) at every geometric point, which is $1$-dimensional. That positive dimension is exactly why $B\mathbb{G}_m$ fails to be DM.

q: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is the correct order of steps in proving that an Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ with an étale atlas is Deligne-Mumford? The standard proof: (1) representability of the diagonal is required for any Artin stack; (2) if the diagonal is also unramified, automorphism groups are finite and unramified (0-dimensional); (3) this is equivalent to the existence of an étale atlas, the defining condition of DM. Find an atlas, then check fibers are etale.

q: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford?
text: Which is a counterexample showing that $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is not Deligne-Mumford? A stack is DM iff its diagonal is unramified (equivalently, automorphism schemes are 0-dimensional). Since $\mathbb{G}_m$ is 1-dimensional, the inertia stack $I(B\mathbb{G}_m)\cong[\mathbb{G}_m/\mathbb{G}_m]$ has positive-dimensional fibers, so $B\mathbb{G}_m$ is Artin but not DM. The inertia group $\mathbb{G}_m$ is positive-dimensional, failing DM.

q: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$?
text: An Artin stack $\mathcal{X}$ has a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ with $U$ a scheme of dimension $3$ and the fibers of the atlas map have dimension $1$. What is $\dim(\mathcal{X})$? For a smooth atlas $U\to\mathcal{X}$ of relative dimension $d$, $\dim(\mathcal{X})=\dim(U)-d=3-1=2$. Here the fiber dimension $1$ corresponds to the $1$-dimensional automorphism group typical of an Artin (non-DM) stack. Dimension of the stack is atlas dimension minus fiber dimension.

q: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack?
text: Which condition separates a Deligne-Mumford stack from a general Artin (algebraic) stack? The defining upgrade from Artin to DM is the étale (versus merely smooth) atlas, which forces automorphism schemes to be finite and unramified. $BG$ for $G$ finite is DM; $BG$ for $G$ positive-dimensional (e.g. $B\mathbb{G}_m$) is Artin but not DM.

q: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true?
text: Keel-Mori produces a coarse moduli map $\pi:\mathcal{X}\to X$ for a finite-type DM stack with finite inertia. Which statement about $\pi$ is true? $\pi$ is a bijection on geometric points and any scheme-valued map from $\mathcal{X}$ factors uniquely through $\pi$. But the fiber of $\pi$ over a stacky point is a classifying stack $B\mathrm{Aut}$, so automorphism data is discarded; e.g. $\mathrm{CH}^*(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})_{\mathbb{Z}}=\mathbb{Z}[t]/(12t)\ne \mathrm{CH}^*(\mathbb{A}^1)$.

q: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)?
text: For $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ over $\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathbb{Z}[1/6]$, what is the order of the automorphism group of the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3+1$ (the $j=0$ point)? At $j=0$, $E$ has complex multiplication by $\mathbb{Z}[\zeta_3]$ and $\mathrm{Aut}(E)=\mu_6$, of order $6$. After quotienting by the generic $\mu_2$ ($[-1]$), residual automorphism is $\mu_3$, so locally $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ looks like $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_3]$ near $j=0$.

q: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is:
text: The correct sequence of steps in constructing the coarse moduli space via Keel-Mori is: Keel-Mori: after checking finite inertia and finite type, one locally reduces to a $\mu_n$-gerbe over an algebraic space, handles the tame case via étale-local arguments, then glues the local coarse spaces using the étale topology to produce a global algebraic space (or scheme in good cases). Construct the space, then check universal property.

q: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings?
text: Which is a counterexample showing the coarse space map $\pi:\mathcal{M}_{1,1}\to\mathbb{A}^1_j$ does NOT preserve integral Chow rings? The Hodge line bundle $\lambda$ on $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ satisfies $12\lambda=0$ in $\mathrm{Pic}(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})$ (reflecting the $\mu_2$ automorphism at every point and the $\mu_6$ at $j=0$), giving $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathcal{M}_{1,1})\cong\mathbb{Z}/12$. Meanwhile $\mathrm{CH}^1(\mathbb{A}^1)=0$, so $\pi^*$ is not an isomorphism integrally. Coarse spaces have different integral classes at orbifold points.

q: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group?
text: For the DM stack $[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ (with $\mu_n$ acting by scaling), the coarse moduli map $\pi:[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]\to\mathbb{A}^1$ sends the stacky point $0$ to $0$. The local Picard group of the stack at $0$ is $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$, what is the order of this local Picard group? The stacky point $0\in[\mathbb{A}^1/\mu_n]$ has automorphism group $\mu_n$, contributing a local $\mathrm{Pic}\cong\widehat{\mu_n}\cong\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. For $n=4$ this has order $4$. The coarse map is degree one but ramifies at the origin.

title: Hasse bound and the Frobenius angle
blurb: For E/Q with good reduction at p, write a_p = 2√p · cos θ_p with θ_p ∈ [0, π]. The Frobenius eigenvalues are e^{±iθ_p}√p on the unit circle (after rescaling).

title: Equidistribution and Weyl's criterion
blurb: A sequence on a compact space equidistributes with respect to a measure μ iff prime-averaged characters of every non-trivial irreducible tend to 0 — the Weyl/Wiener–Ikehara framework that Sato–Tate fits into.

title: The Sato–Tate measure
blurb: For non-CM E, the angles θ_p equidistribute on [0, π] with respect to the semicircular density (2/π) sin²θ dθ.

title: CM vs. non-CM: two equidistribution laws
blurb: CM curves equidistribute against the Haar measure of the normalizer of a torus in SU(2) — half the primes are supersingular and the rest fill [0,π] uniformly — while non-CM curves follow the full semicircle law.

title: Symmetric-power L-functions
blurb: Sato–Tate is equivalent to non-vanishing and holomorphy of L(Sym^n E, s) on Re(s) ≥ 1 for every n ≥ 1.

q: For $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of good reduction at $p = 11$, suppose $a_{11} = 4$. Compute $\theta_{11}$ in radians (principal value in $[0,\pi]$), rounded to 3 decimals.
text: For $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of good reduction at $p = 11$, suppose $a_{11} = 4$. Compute $\theta_{11}$ in radians (principal value in $[0,\pi]$), rounded to 3 decimals. $a_p = 2\sqrt{p}\cos\theta_p$ gives $\cos\theta_{11} = 4/(2\sqrt{11}) = 2/\sqrt{11} \approx 0.6030$, so $\theta_{11} = \arccos(0.6030) \approx 0.9281$.

q: At a prime $p$ of good reduction, the Frobenius eigenvalues $\alpha_p, \beta_p$ satisfy $\alpha_p + \beta_p = a_p$ and $\alpha_p\beta_p = p$. Writing them as $\sqrt{p}\,e^{\pm i\theta_p}$, a supersingular prime is one for which:
text: At a prime $p$ of good reduction, the Frobenius eigenvalues $\alpha_p, \beta_p$ satisfy $\alpha_p + \beta_p = a_p$ and $\alpha_p\beta_p = p$. Writing them as $\sqrt{p}\,e^{\pm i\theta_p}$, a supersingular prime is one for which: Supersingular means $a_p = 0$, i.e. $\cos\theta_p = 0$, i.e. $\theta_p = \pi/2$. The eigenvalues are then $\pm i\sqrt{p}$.

q: The Hasse bound $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$ forces $\theta_p$ to lie in which interval? Enter the length of that interval.
text: The Hasse bound $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$ forces $\theta_p$ to lie in which interval? Enter the length of that interval. The map $\theta \mapsto 2\sqrt{p}\cos\theta$ is a bijection $[0,\pi] \to [-2\sqrt{p}, 2\sqrt{p}]$, so $\theta_p \in [0,\pi]$, of length $\pi$.

q: For the elliptic curve $y^2=x^3-x$ at $p=5$, one finds $a_5=-2$. Compute $\theta_5\in[0,\pi]$ in radians, rounded to 3 decimals. (Use $\cos\theta_5 = a_5/(2\sqrt{5})$.)
text: For the elliptic curve $y^2=x^3-x$ at $p=5$, one finds $a_5=-2$. Compute $\theta_5\in[0,\pi]$ in radians, rounded to 3 decimals. (Use $\cos\theta_5 = a_5/(2\sqrt{5})$.) $\cos\theta_5 = -2/(2\sqrt{5}) = -1/\sqrt{5}\approx -0.4472$, so $\theta_5 = \arccos(-0.4472)\approx 2.034$. Check: $2\sqrt{5}\cos(2.034)\approx 2(2.236)(-0.4472)\approx -2$. ✓

q: Which of the following is a correct counterexample showing the Hasse bound $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ is tight, i.e., $|a_p|$ can equal $2\sqrt{p}$ exactly only in a degenerate case?
text: Which of the following is a correct counterexample showing the Hasse bound $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ is tight, i.e., $|a_p|$ can equal $2\sqrt{p}$ exactly only in a degenerate case? Since $a_p$ is an integer and $2\sqrt{p}$ is irrational for any prime $p$, the strict inequality $|a_p|<2\sqrt{p}$ holds for every prime of good reduction. The bound is asymptotically tight in the sense that $a_p/(2\sqrt{p})$ is dense in $[-1,1]$, but never exactly $\pm 1$.

q: What is the correct chain of logical implications that produced the Frobenius angle $\theta_p$?
text: What is the correct chain of logical implications that produced the Frobenius angle $\theta_p$? Hasse proved $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$ directly; the Weil conjectures (proved by Deligne for curves) imply $|\alpha_p|=|\beta_p|=\sqrt{p}$. Writing $\alpha_p=\sqrt{p}e^{i\theta_p}$ then gives $a_p=\alpha_p+\beta_p=2\sqrt{p}\cos\theta_p$.

q: Weyl's criterion says a sequence $(x_n)$ in $[0,1]$ equidistributes w.r.t. Lebesgue measure iff for every integer $k\ne 0$:
text: Weyl's criterion says a sequence $(x_n)$ in $[0,1]$ equidistributes w.r.t. Lebesgue measure iff for every integer $k\ne 0$: Weyl's criterion: equidistribution with respect to a measure $\mu$ on a compact space is equivalent to prime-averaged matrix coefficients of every non-trivial irreducible representation tending to $0$. On $\mathbb{R}/\mathbb{Z}$ the non-trivial irreducibles are the characters $e^{2\pi i k\cdot}$ for $k\ne 0$.

q: For Sato–Tate, the compact space is $\mathrm{SU}(2)/\mathrm{conj}\cong[0,\pi]$ and the 'characters' that must average to $0$ are:
text: For Sato–Tate, the compact space is $\mathrm{SU}(2)/\mathrm{conj}\cong[0,\pi]$ and the 'characters' that must average to $0$ are: Non-trivial irreducibles of $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ are exactly $\mathrm{Sym}^n$ for $n\ge 1$, with character $\chi_n(\theta)=\sin((n+1)\theta)/\sin\theta$. Equidistribution of $\theta_p$ is equivalent to $\tfrac{1}{\pi(X)}\sum_{p\le X}\chi_n(\theta_p)\to 0$ for every $n\ge 1$, the content of Sato–Tate.

q: Compute $\int_0^\pi \chi_1(\theta)\,d\mu_{ST}(\theta)$ where $\chi_1(\theta)=\sin(2\theta)/\sin\theta=2\cos\theta$ and $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$.
text: Compute $\int_0^\pi \chi_1(\theta)\,d\mu_{ST}(\theta)$ where $\chi_1(\theta)=\sin(2\theta)/\sin\theta=2\cos\theta$ and $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$. $\int_0^\pi 2\cos\theta\cdot(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = (4/\pi)\int_0^\pi \cos\theta\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = (4/\pi)\cdot 0 = 0$ (the integrand is antisymmetric about $\theta=\pi/2$). This is orthogonality of $\chi_1$ to the trivial representation on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$.

q: Weyl's criterion for $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ requires $\frac{1}{\pi(X)}\sum_{p\le X}\chi_n(\theta_p)\to 0$ for every $n\ge 1$. If one knows this only for $n=1$, which conclusion is valid?
text: Weyl's criterion for $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ requires $\frac{1}{\pi(X)}\sum_{p\le X}\chi_n(\theta_p)\to 0$ for every $n\ge 1$. If one knows this only for $n=1$, which conclusion is valid? Each $n$ in Weyl's criterion is an independent condition. Knowing $n=1$ controls only the first trigonometric moment $\langle\cos\theta\rangle$. Full equidistribution requires all $n\ge 1$, corresponding to holomorphy and non-vanishing of every $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E,s)$ on $\operatorname{Re}s\ge 1$. Each $n$ is an independent moment condition.

q: Compute $\int_0^\pi \chi_2(\theta)\,d\mu_{ST}(\theta)$ where $\chi_2(\theta)=4\cos^2\theta-1$ is the character of $\mathrm{Sym}^2$. Use $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$.
text: Compute $\int_0^\pi \chi_2(\theta)\,d\mu_{ST}(\theta)$ where $\chi_2(\theta)=4\cos^2\theta-1$ is the character of $\mathrm{Sym}^2$. Use $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$. $\int_0^\pi(4\cos^2\theta-1)\tfrac{2}{\pi}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \tfrac{2}{\pi}\bigl[4\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta\,d\theta - \int_0^\pi\sin^2\theta\,d\theta\bigr] = \tfrac{2}{\pi}\bigl[4\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{8} - \tfrac{\pi}{2}\bigr] = \tfrac{2}{\pi}(\tfrac{\pi}{2}-\tfrac{\pi}{2})=0$. This reflects orthogonality of $\mathrm{Sym}^2$ to the trivial rep.

q: Weyl's equidistribution theorem for the sequence $\alpha n\pmod{1}$ ($\alpha$ irrational) uses the same criterion as Sato–Tate. Which is the correct chain of steps?
text: Weyl's equidistribution theorem for the sequence $\alpha n\pmod{1}$ ($\alpha$ irrational) uses the same criterion as Sato–Tate. Which is the correct chain of steps? For $k\ne 0$, $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^Ne^{2\pi ik\alpha n} = \frac{e^{2\pi ik\alpha}(e^{2\pi ikN\alpha}-1)}{N(e^{2\pi ik\alpha}-1)}\to 0$ since the denominator is bounded away from zero when $\alpha\notin\mathbb{Q}$. The criterion then yields equidistribution by approximating indicator functions with trigonometric polynomials.

q: The Sato–Tate density on $[0,\pi]$ is:
text: The Sato–Tate density on $[0,\pi]$ is: The conjugacy measure on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ pushed down to $[0,\pi]$ is $(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$, integrating to $1$.

q: Under the Sato–Tate measure, compute $\Pr\!\left[0 \le \theta \le \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$.
text: Under the Sato–Tate measure, compute $\Pr\!\left[0 \le \theta \le \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$. $\int_0^{\pi/2}\tfrac{2}{\pi}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = \tfrac{2}{\pi}\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \tfrac12$. The density is symmetric about $\theta = \pi/2$.

q: For a non-CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the Sato–Tate conjecture (now theorem of Barnet-Lamb–Geraghty–Harris–Taylor) says:
text: For a non-CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the Sato–Tate conjecture (now theorem of Barnet-Lamb–Geraghty–Harris–Taylor) says: Equidistribution: for any continuous test function $f$ on $[0,\pi]$, $\lim_{X\to\infty}\tfrac{1}{\pi(X)}\sum_{p\le X} f(\theta_p) = \int_0^\pi f\,d\mu_{ST}$.

q: Compute the variance of $\cos\theta$ under $\mu_{ST}$, i.e., $\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\,d\mu_{ST} - \left(\int_0^\pi\cos\theta\,d\mu_{ST}\right)^2$. Use $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$.
text: Compute the variance of $\cos\theta$ under $\mu_{ST}$, i.e., $\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\,d\mu_{ST} - \left(\int_0^\pi\cos\theta\,d\mu_{ST}\right)^2$. Use $d\mu_{ST}=(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$. $\int_0^\pi\cos\theta\,d\mu_{ST}=0$ (shown earlier). $\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\,(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = (2/\pi)\int_0^\pi\cos^2\theta\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = (2/\pi)\cdot\pi/8 = 1/4$. Variance $= 1/4 - 0 = 1/4$.

q: The Sato–Tate measure is the pushforward of normalized Haar measure on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ along the map $g\mapsto\theta_g$ (half the argument of eigenvalues). Which statement about this measure is FALSE?
text: The Sato–Tate measure is the pushforward of normalized Haar measure on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ along the map $g\mapsto\theta_g$ (half the argument of eigenvalues). Which statement about this measure is FALSE? The Sato–Tate density is $(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta$, not the uniform measure $1/\pi$. It peaks at $\theta=\pi/2$ and vanishes at the endpoints, so options A, B, D are true statements.

q: Under $\mu_{ST}$, what is the probability that $a_p > 0$, i.e., that $\theta_p\in(0,\pi/2)$?
text: Under $\mu_{ST}$, what is the probability that $a_p > 0$, i.e., that $\theta_p\in(0,\pi/2)$? $\Pr[\theta_p\in(0,\pi/2)]=\int_0^{\pi/2}(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta\,d\theta = (2/\pi)\cdot\pi/4 = 1/2$. The density $(2/\pi)\sin^2\theta$ is symmetric about $\theta=\pi/2$, so exactly half the mass lies in each half.

q: For a CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the Frobenius angles $\theta_p$ equidistribute against:
text: For a CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the Frobenius angles $\theta_p$ equidistribute against: For CM curves, half the primes (those inert in the CM field $K$) are supersingular, giving $a_p=0$ and $\theta_p=\pi/2$. The other half (split in $K$) follow the Haar measure on the split torus of $\mathrm{U}(1)\subset\mathrm{SU}(2)$, which on $[0,\pi]$ is the uniform measure $d\theta/\pi$.

q: The Sato–Tate group of a non-CM elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$ is:
text: The Sato–Tate group of a non-CM elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$ is: Non-CM: the image of Galois in $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_\ell)$ is open (Serre), which after normalization gives the full compact group $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ as Sato–Tate group. CM: the Galois image sits inside a non-split torus, so the Sato–Tate group is the normalizer $N(T)$; the two cosets of $T$ in $N(T)$ are the split and non-split (supersingular) primes.

q: For a CM curve, the density of supersingular primes is $1/2$. Given $\pi(10^6)=78{,}498$, estimate how many of the first $\pi(10^6)$ primes would be supersingular for a CM curve with good reduction everywhere.
text: For a CM curve, the density of supersingular primes is $1/2$. Given $\pi(10^6)=78{,}498$, estimate how many of the first $\pi(10^6)$ primes would be supersingular for a CM curve with good reduction everywhere. Half of $78498$ is $39249$. The exact count fluctuates by $O(\sqrt{\pi(X)})$ — Chebotarev with error term — so any count within a few hundred is consistent with the CM equidistribution law.

q: For a CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with CM by $\mathcal{O}_K$, the Sato–Tate group is $N(T)$, the normalizer of a maximal torus $T\cong\mathrm{U}(1)$ in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$. Which is the correct order-of-steps argument showing the equidistribution for split primes?
text: For a CM elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with CM by $\mathcal{O}_K$, the Sato–Tate group is $N(T)$, the normalizer of a maximal torus $T\cong\mathrm{U}(1)$ in $\mathrm{SU}(2)$. Which is the correct order-of-steps argument showing the equidistribution for split primes? For split primes $p$ in a CM curve, $a_p=\psi(\mathfrak{p})+\overline{\psi(\mathfrak{p})}$ for a Hecke Grössencharacter $\psi$. The associated Hecke $L$-function is entire and non-vanishing on $\operatorname{Re}s\ge 1$, so the split angles equidistribute uniformly on $[0,\pi]$ (the Haar measure on $\mathrm{U}(1)$ pushed to $[0,\pi]$). For split primes in a CM curve, Frobenius comes from a Grossencharacter, not from $\mathrm{SU}(2)$.

q: Which space is NOT a valid Sato–Tate group for any motive over $\mathbb{Q}$ of rank 2?
text: Which space is NOT a valid Sato–Tate group for any motive over $\mathbb{Q}$ of rank 2? $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ is not a subgroup of $\mathrm{USp}(2)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)$ in the relevant sense for rank-2 motives. The Sato–Tate groups for rank-2 motives are $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ (non-CM), $N(T)$ (CM over $\mathbb{Q}$), or $U(1)$ (CM over the base field); $\mathrm{SO}(3)$ appears for symmetric-square motives, not rank-2.

q: For a CM curve with CM field $K=\mathbb{Q}(i)$, primes that split in $K$ are those $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$. By Dirichlet's theorem, their density among all primes is $1/2$. Given $\pi(100)=25$, estimate the count of primes $p\le 100$ with $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$.
text: For a CM curve with CM field $K=\mathbb{Q}(i)$, primes that split in $K$ are those $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$. By Dirichlet's theorem, their density among all primes is $1/2$. Given $\pi(100)=25$, estimate the count of primes $p\le 100$ with $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$. Primes $\le 100$ with $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$: $5,13,17,29,37,41,53,61,73,89,97$ — that is $11$ primes. Dirichlet predicts roughly $25/2\approx 12.5$; the actual count is $11$.

q: The local Euler factor of $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E, s)$ at a good prime $p$ is:
text: The local Euler factor of $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E, s)$ at a good prime $p$ is: $\mathrm{Sym}^n$ of the 2-dim Frobenius rep has eigenvalues $\alpha_p^{n-k}\beta_p^k$ for $k=0,\ldots,n$, giving $n+1$ local inverse-linear factors.

q: If $\theta_p = \pi/3$ (so $\alpha_p = \sqrt{p}\,e^{i\pi/3}$), compute $\alpha_p^2 + \alpha_p\beta_p + \beta_p^2$ divided by $p$. (This is the trace of $\mathrm{Sym}^2$ Frobenius divided by $p$.)
text: If $\theta_p = \pi/3$ (so $\alpha_p = \sqrt{p}\,e^{i\pi/3}$), compute $\alpha_p^2 + \alpha_p\beta_p + \beta_p^2$ divided by $p$. (This is the trace of $\mathrm{Sym}^2$ Frobenius divided by $p$.) $\alpha_p^2 + \beta_p^2 = a_p^2 - 2p = 4p\cos^2\theta_p - 2p$, and $\alpha_p\beta_p = p$. Sum = $4p\cos^2\theta_p - p$. Dividing by $p$: $4\cos^2(\pi/3) - 1 = 4\cdot\tfrac14 - 1 = 0$ … plus $\alpha\beta/p = 1$, giving $0 + 1 + 1 = 2$. (Equivalently: $\sum e^{i(2-2k)\theta_p} = U_2(\cos\theta_p) = 4\cos^2\theta_p - 1 + 1 = 2$ at $\theta = \pi/3$.)

q: Sato–Tate equidistribution follows from which analytic property?
text: Sato–Tate equidistribution follows from which analytic property? By a Tauberian / Wiener–Ikehara argument, non-vanishing + holomorphy of each $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E, s)$ on $\mathrm{Re}\,s \ge 1$ yields equidistribution of traces with respect to the Sato–Tate measure via character orthogonality on $\mathrm{SU}(2)$.

q: For $\theta_p=\pi/4$, compute the trace of $\mathrm{Sym}^3$ Frobenius: $\alpha_p^3+\alpha_p^2\beta_p+\alpha_p\beta_p^2+\beta_p^3$, divided by $p^{3/2}$.
text: For $\theta_p=\pi/4$, compute the trace of $\mathrm{Sym}^3$ Frobenius: $\alpha_p^3+\alpha_p^2\beta_p+\alpha_p\beta_p^2+\beta_p^3$, divided by $p^{3/2}$. The $\mathrm{Sym}^3$ character is $\chi_3(\theta)=\sin(4\theta)/\sin\theta$. At $\theta=\pi/4$: $\sin(\pi)=0$, so $\chi_3(\pi/4)=0$. Alternatively, $U_3(\cos(\pi/4))=U_3(1/\sqrt2)=4(1/\sqrt2)^3-2(1/\sqrt2)=4/(2\sqrt2)-\sqrt2=\sqrt2-\sqrt2=0$.

q: The proof of Sato–Tate by Barnet-Lamb–Geraghty–Harris–Taylor establishes holomorphy and non-vanishing of $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E,s)$ on $\operatorname{Re}s\ge 1$ by which main strategy?
text: The proof of Sato–Tate by Barnet-Lamb–Geraghty–Harris–Taylor establishes holomorphy and non-vanishing of $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E,s)$ on $\operatorname{Re}s\ge 1$ by which main strategy? BLGHT uses the 'potential automorphy' method of Taylor: one shows that $\mathrm{Sym}^n(\rho_{E,\ell})$ becomes automorphic over some totally real field $F$ (a solvable base change), establishes the required analytic properties there, and then uses automorphic induction / descent to get non-vanishing on $\operatorname{Re}s\ge 1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$. Prove what you want over a solvable base change, then descend.

q: Which chain of inclusions correctly describes the local Euler factor degree for $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E,s)$ versus $L(E,s)$?
text: Which chain of inclusions correctly describes the local Euler factor degree for $L(\mathrm{Sym}^n E,s)$ versus $L(E,s)$? $L(E,s)$ has $(1-\alpha_p p^{-s})^{-1}(1-\beta_p p^{-s})^{-1}$ at good $p$: degree 2. $\mathrm{Sym}^n$ of the 2-dim Frobenius has eigenvalues $\{\alpha_p^{n-k}\beta_p^k\}_{k=0}^n$, giving $n+1$ inverse-linear factors, so degree $n+1$.

title: Mordell–Weil theorem
blurb: E(Q) is finitely generated: E(Q) ≅ Z^r ⊕ E(Q)_tors. The rank r is a deep invariant; the torsion is tame (Mazur).

title: Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer heuristic
blurb: Numerical origin of BSD: ∏_{p≤X} N_p/p ≈ c·(log X)^r for the true rank r. Curves of higher rank have more points modulo p on average.

title: BSD rank equality
blurb: The conjecture: ord_{s=1} L(E, s) = rank E(Q). Sharper form predicts the leading Taylor coefficient in terms of regulator, Sha, periods, torsion.

title: Néron–Tate height and the regulator
blurb: The canonical height ĥ is a positive-definite quadratic form on E(Q)/tors; its Gram determinant on a basis is the regulator R, the volume of the Mordell–Weil lattice that enters the refined BSD formula.

title: The Tate–Shafarevich group Ш
blurb: Ш(E/Q) measures the failure of the local-to-global principle for torsors under E. Conjecturally finite, its order enters the refined BSD formula as a multiplicative fudge factor.

q: Mordell–Weil says that for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the group $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is:
text: Mordell–Weil says that for an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$, the group $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is: The theorem gives a decomposition $E(\mathbb{Q}) \cong \mathbb{Z}^r \oplus T$ where $T$ is a finite torsion group and $r \ge 0$ is the rank.

q: By Mazur's theorem, how many isomorphism classes of torsion subgroups $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ are possible?
text: By Mazur's theorem, how many isomorphism classes of torsion subgroups $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ are possible? Mazur (1977): the torsion is one of $\mathbb{Z}/n$ for $n\in\{1,\ldots,10,12\}$ (11 groups) or $\mathbb{Z}/2\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2n$ for $n\in\{1,2,3,4\}$ (4 groups), total 15.

q: For the congruent-number curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - 25x$, the rank is $1$ and the torsion is $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. How many rational torsion points does $E(\mathbb{Q})$ contain (including $O$)?
text: For the congruent-number curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - 25x$, the rank is $1$ and the torsion is $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. How many rational torsion points does $E(\mathbb{Q})$ contain (including $O$)? $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ has order $4$; the non-identity $2$-torsion points are $(0,0), (5,0), (-5,0)$ plus the identity at infinity.

q: Weak Mordell–Weil shows $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})$ is finite. Which tool reduces the infinite group $E(\mathbb{Q})$ to a finite computation from this fact?
text: Weak Mordell–Weil shows $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})$ is finite. Which tool reduces the infinite group $E(\mathbb{Q})$ to a finite computation from this fact? Tate’s canonical height $\hat h$ is quadratic: $\hat h(nP)=n^2\hat h(P)$. Finiteness of $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})$ plus the fact that $\{P : \hat h(P)\le B\}$ is finite for every $B$ lets one descend an arbitrary point to a coset representative, proving finite generation. You need a size function that behaves predictably under doubling.

q: The curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ has rank $0$ and $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}} \cong (\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. The doubling map $[2]: E(\mathbb{Q}) \to E(\mathbb{Q})$ has image:
text: The curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x$ has rank $0$ and $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}} \cong (\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$. The doubling map $[2]: E(\mathbb{Q}) \to E(\mathbb{Q})$ has image: Every non-identity element of $(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ satisfies $2P = O$. So $[2]$ kills the entire group and its image is $\{O\}$.

q: The free part of $E(\mathbb{Q})$ for a rank-$3$ curve has a Gram matrix (height pairing) with diagonal entries $(2, 3, 5)$ and all off-diagonal entries $0$. Compute the regulator $R$.
text: The free part of $E(\mathbb{Q})$ for a rank-$3$ curve has a Gram matrix (height pairing) with diagonal entries $(2, 3, 5)$ and all off-diagonal entries $0$. Compute the regulator $R$. With a diagonal Gram matrix $\operatorname{diag}(2,3,5)$, the determinant is $2\cdot 3\cdot 5 = 30$. Orthogonality of the basis means each generator contributes independently to the lattice covolume.

q: BSD's original numerical heuristic predicts that $\prod_{p \le X} \dfrac{N_p}{p}$ grows like:
text: BSD's original numerical heuristic predicts that $\prod_{p \le X} \dfrac{N_p}{p}$ grows like: Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer observed on EDSAC that the product grows like $c\cdot(\log X)^r$, where $r$ is the rank of $E(\mathbb{Q})$.

q: Here $N_p = \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$. Higher rank corresponds to curves having, on average:
text: Here $N_p = \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$. Higher rank corresponds to curves having, on average: Heuristically, lots of rational points forces many points modulo $p$. This bias in $a_p = p+1-N_p$ (systematically negative in some average sense) is the statistical shadow of positive rank.

q: By Hasse's bound, $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$. For $p = 25$ this would give $|a_p| \le ?$ (take $p$ as a real number here, ignoring that $25$ is not prime).
text: By Hasse's bound, $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$. For $p = 25$ this would give $|a_p| \le ?$ (take $p$ as a real number here, ignoring that $25$ is not prime). $2\sqrt{25} = 10$. The Hasse bound controls each factor $N_p/p = 1 - a_p/p + 1/p$ so it lies in $[1 - 2/\sqrt{p} + 1/p,\ 1 + 2/\sqrt{p} + 1/p]$.

q: For the Euler product $L(E,s) = \prod_{p\nmid N}(1-a_p p^{-s}+p^{1-2s})^{-1}\cdots$, the quantity $a_p = p+1-N_p$ satisfies $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$ (Hasse). When $E$ has positive rank, which qualitative statement about the Dirichlet coefficients is behind the heuristic?
text: For the Euler product $L(E,s) = \prod_{p\nmid N}(1-a_p p^{-s}+p^{1-2s})^{-1}\cdots$, the quantity $a_p = p+1-N_p$ satisfies $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt{p}$ (Hasse). When $E$ has positive rank, which qualitative statement about the Dirichlet coefficients is behind the heuristic? Higher rank pushes more rational points; reducing mod $p$ they persist and bias $N_p$ above $p+1$, making $a_p = p+1-N_p$ systematically negative on average. This is the heuristic reason $\prod_{p\le X}N_p/p$ grows rather than stays bounded.

q: For $E: y^2+y = x^3-x^2$ (conductor $11$, rank $0$) the formula $N_{11} = 11+1-a_{11}$ applies with $a_{11} = 1$ (good reduction is lost; treat as $a_{11}=1$ here). Compute $N_{11}$.
text: For $E: y^2+y = x^3-x^2$ (conductor $11$, rank $0$) the formula $N_{11} = 11+1-a_{11}$ applies with $a_{11} = 1$ (good reduction is lost; treat as $a_{11}=1$ here). Compute $N_{11}$. $N_{11} = 11+1-1 = 11$. (The conductor is $11$ so $11$ is a prime of bad reduction; the exact Euler factor differs, but the arithmetic $11+1-1=11$ follows directly from the given data.)

q: The Sato–Tate conjecture (now theorem) describes the distribution of $a_p/(2\sqrt{p})$ as $p\to\infty$. What is that distribution?
text: The Sato–Tate conjecture (now theorem) describes the distribution of $a_p/(2\sqrt{p})$ as $p\to\infty$. What is that distribution? Sato–Tate (proved by Taylor et al., 2011): for a non-CM elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$ the angles $\theta_p$ with $a_p = 2\sqrt{p}\cos\theta_p$ equidistribute with respect to the Haar measure $\frac{2}{\pi}\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$, which translates to the semicircle on $[-1,1]$.

q: The (weak) Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture asserts:
text: The (weak) Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture asserts: BSD equates the analytic rank (order of vanishing of $L(E,s)$ at $s=1$) with the algebraic rank of the Mordell–Weil group.

q: For a rank-$0$ elliptic curve, BSD predicts that $L(E, 1)$ equals zero ($\to$ enter $0$) or is nonzero ($\to$ enter $1$)?
text: For a rank-$0$ elliptic curve, BSD predicts that $L(E, 1)$ equals zero ($\to$ enter $0$) or is nonzero ($\to$ enter $1$)? Rank $0$ means $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1} L(E,s) = 0$, i.e. $L(E,1) \ne 0$. Kolyvagin's theorem proves the BSD implication in this direction for modular $E$.

q: The full BSD conjecture expresses $\lim_{s\to 1} \dfrac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r}$ in terms of which invariants?
text: The full BSD conjecture expresses $\lim_{s\to 1} \dfrac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r}$ in terms of which invariants? Refined BSD: $\displaystyle \lim_{s\to 1}\frac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r} = \frac{\Omega \cdot R \cdot |\Sha| \cdot \prod_p c_p}{|E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}|^2}.$

q: Gross–Zagier (1986) relates $L'(E,1)$ (when analytic rank $=1$) to what explicit algebraic object?
text: Gross–Zagier (1986) relates $L'(E,1)$ (when analytic rank $=1$) to what explicit algebraic object? Gross–Zagier: $L'(E,1) = \frac{\Omega\cdot c}{\sqrt{|\Delta_K|}}\cdot\hat h(y_K)$ for a Heegner point $y_K$ on $E(K)$. Combined with Kolyvagin’s Euler systems this proves BSD for analytic rank $1$.

q: Kolyvagin’s theorem (1989) establishes BSD in which cases?
text: Kolyvagin’s theorem (1989) establishes BSD in which cases? Kolyvagin proved: if $E/\mathbb{Q}$ is modular (now all curves, by Wiles et al.) and $\operatorname{ord}_{s=1}L(E,s) \le 1$, then the algebraic rank equals the analytic rank and $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite. The case of analytic rank $\ge 2$ remains open.

q:
Order the following steps in the proof strategy for BSD rank $=1$: (A) Use Gross–Zagier to show $\hat h(y_K) 
e 0$ from $L'(E,1) 
e 0$. (B) Apply Kolyvagin’s Euler system to deduce $\operatorname{rank}E(\mathbb{Q})=1$. (C) Verify analytic rank $= 1$ via $L(E,1)=0$, $L'(E,1)
e 0$. What is the correct order?
text:
Order the following steps in the proof strategy for BSD rank $=1$: (A) Use Gross–Zagier to show $\hat h(y_K) 
e 0$ from $L'(E,1) 
e 0$. (B) Apply Kolyvagin’s Euler system to deduce $\operatorname{rank}E(\mathbb{Q})=1$. (C) Verify analytic rank $= 1$ via $L(E,1)=0$, $L'(E,1)
e 0$. What is the correct order? First establish the analytic data $L(E,1)=0$, $L'(E,1)
e 0$ (step C). Then Gross–Zagier converts $L'(E,1)
e 0$ into $\hat h(y_K)
e 0$, so the Heegner point is non-torsion (step A). Finally Kolyvagin’s machinery propagates this to algebraic rank exactly $1$ (step B). Analytic data comes first; then convert it to a non-torsion point; then use Euler systems.

q: The Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ satisfies which scaling law?
text: The Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ satisfies which scaling law? Tate's limiting construction makes $\hat h$ exactly quadratic, so $\hat h(nP)=n^2\hat h(P)$. Equivalently, $\langle P,Q\rangle = \tfrac12(\hat h(P+Q)-\hat h(P)-\hat h(Q))$ is bilinear.

q: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $2$ with height-pairing Gram matrix $\begin{pmatrix}2 & 1\\ 1 & 3\end{pmatrix}$ on a basis. Compute the regulator $R=\det G$.
text: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $2$ with height-pairing Gram matrix $\begin{pmatrix}2 & 1\\ 1 & 3\end{pmatrix}$ on a basis. Compute the regulator $R=\det G$. $R = 2\cdot 3 - 1\cdot 1 = 5$. Positive-definiteness (so the pairing is a genuine inner product) requires $\det G > 0$, which holds here.

q: By convention, the regulator of a rank-$0$ elliptic curve is:
text: By convention, the regulator of a rank-$0$ elliptic curve is: The free part is zero-dimensional, so the Gram matrix is $0\times 0$ and its determinant is $1$. This keeps refined BSD sensible when $r=0$: the formula reduces to $L(E,1) = \Omega\cdot |\Sha|\cdot\prod c_p / |E_{\text{tors}}|^2$.

q: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $1$ with generator $P$ satisfying $\hat h(P) = 0.5051$. Compute $\hat h(3P)$.
text: Let $E(\mathbb{Q})$ have rank $1$ with generator $P$ satisfying $\hat h(P) = 0.5051$. Compute $\hat h(3P)$. $\hat h(3P) = 3^2 \cdot \hat h(P) = 9 \times 0.5051 = 4.5459$. The canonical height is exactly quadratic: $\hat h(nP)=n^2\hat h(P)$.

q: Which property distinguishes the Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ from the naive Weil height $h$ of the $x$-coordinate?
text: Which property distinguishes the Néron–Tate canonical height $\hat h$ from the naive Weil height $h$ of the $x$-coordinate? The Weil height $h(P)$ satisfies $h(nP) = n^2 h(P) + O(1)$. Tate’s limiting process $\hat h(P) = \lim_{n\to\infty} h(2^n P)/4^n$ removes the $O(1)$ error, making $\hat h$ exactly quadratic. Crucially, $\hat h(P)=0$ iff $P$ is torsion.

q: A rank-$2$ curve has height-pairing matrix $\begin{pmatrix}4 & 2\\ 2 & 4\end{pmatrix}$. Is this matrix positive definite? (Enter $1$ for yes, $0$ for no.)
text: A rank-$2$ curve has height-pairing matrix $\begin{pmatrix}4 & 2\\ 2 & 4\end{pmatrix}$. Is this matrix positive definite? (Enter $1$ for yes, $0$ for no.) Check: $\det = 4\cdot 4 - 2\cdot 2 = 12 > 0$ and the leading entry $4 > 0$, so Sylvester’s criterion confirms positive definiteness. The canonical height pairing on the free part is always positive definite.

q: An element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ corresponds geometrically to:
text: An element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ corresponds geometrically to: $\Sha = \ker\bigl(H^1(\mathbb{Q},E) \to \prod_v H^1(\mathbb{Q}_v,E)\bigr)$ classifies principal homogeneous spaces for $E$ that are everywhere locally trivial but globally nontrivial — failures of the Hasse principle.

q: If $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, Cassels–Tate forces its order to be a perfect square. Which of $\{1, 2, 4, 6, 9\}$ is NOT a possible value of $|\Sha|$? Enter the value.
text: If $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, Cassels–Tate forces its order to be a perfect square. Which of $\{1, 2, 4, 6, 9\}$ is NOT a possible value of $|\Sha|$? Enter the value. $6$ is not a perfect square, so it is forbidden by the Cassels–Tate pairing (which gives $\Sha$ an alternating non-degenerate pairing, hence square order). $1, 4, 9$ are squares; $2$ can occur as $|\Sha[2]|$ but not as $|\Sha|$ — however the question only asks about non-square obstruction, making $6$ the cleanest answer.

q: The $n$-descent sequence reads $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \to \Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \to 0$. If $|\mathrm{Sel}_2(E)| = 32$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E(\mathbb{Q})| = 8$, then $|\Sha(E)[2]|$ is:
text: The $n$-descent sequence reads $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \to \Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \to 0$. If $|\mathrm{Sel}_2(E)| = 32$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E(\mathbb{Q})| = 8$, then $|\Sha(E)[2]|$ is: From the exact sequence, $|\Sha[2]| = |\mathrm{Sel}_2|/|E(\mathbb{Q})/2E| = 32/8 = 4$. This is the standard way $2$-descent pins down $|\Sha[2]|$ even when the full group is out of reach.

q: The Cassels–Tate pairing on $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is alternating and non-degenerate (when $\Sha$ is finite). Which of the following is the correct logical chain forcing $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square?
text: The Cassels–Tate pairing on $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is alternating and non-degenerate (when $\Sha$ is finite). Which of the following is the correct logical chain forcing $|\Sha|$ to be a perfect square? A finite abelian group with a non-degenerate alternating bilinear pairing to $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ is self-dual, and the alternating condition forces it to be isomorphic to $B \oplus B$ for some $B$. Hence $|\Sha| = |B|^2$. An alternating form pairs every element with itself to zero — what does that force on the group?

q: A genus-$1$ curve $C/\mathbb{Q}$ represents a non-trivial element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$. What must be true of $C$?
text: A genus-$1$ curve $C/\mathbb{Q}$ represents a non-trivial element of $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$. What must be true of $C$? $\Sha$ consists of everywhere-locally-trivial torsors: $C$ must be locally solvable (points over every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and $\mathbb{R}$) yet have no global $\mathbb{Q}$-point. This is a pure failure of the Hasse principle, with no single local obstruction.

q: Suppose $|\mathrm{Sel}_3(E)| = 27$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 3$. Using the descent exact sequence $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_3(E) \to \Sha(E)[3] \to 0$, compute $|\Sha(E)[3]|$.
text: Suppose $|\mathrm{Sel}_3(E)| = 27$ and $|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 3$. Using the descent exact sequence $0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathrm{Sel}_3(E) \to \Sha(E)[3] \to 0$, compute $|\Sha(E)[3]|$. From the exact sequence, $|\Sha[3]| = |\mathrm{Sel}_3|/|E(\mathbb{Q})/3E(\mathbb{Q})| = 27/3 = 9$. This is consistent with $|\Sha|$ being a perfect square.

title: The Frey curve
blurb: From a hypothetical solution $a^p + b^p = c^p$ to Fermat, Frey attaches $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x - a^p)(x + b^p)$. Its discriminant is $2^{-8}(abc)^{2p}$: a perfect $2p$-th power away from 2, which makes the curve semistable and forces very mild ramification.

title: Modularity theorem
blurb: Every elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ corresponds to a weight-2 newform $f_E \in S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ with matching Frobenius traces $a_p(E) = a_p(f_E)$. Wiles + Taylor–Wiles (1995) handled the semistable case; BCDT (2001) extended to all $E/\mathbb{Q}$.

title: Ribet's level lowering
blurb: Ribet's $\varepsilon$-theorem: if $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ is modular of level $Np$ and unramified at $p$, it is modular at level $N$. Applied to the Frey curve this collapses the level to $2$, where $S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$ contradicts the Fermat solution.

title: Deformation rings
blurb: Given a residual $\overline\rho\colon G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$, Mazur's universal deformation ring $R_\Sigma$ represents lifts to complete Noetherian local rings with prescribed ramification $\Sigma$; it is the formal moduli of Galois deformations.

title: The R = T theorem
blurb: Wiles' lifting engine identifies $R_\Sigma$ with the localized Hecke algebra $\mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ on weight-2 cusp forms; the Taylor–Wiles patching argument upgrades $R_\Sigma \twoheadrightarrow \mathbb{T}_\Sigma$ to an isomorphism, so every deformation is modular.

q: Let $a,b,c$ be a hypothetical coprime solution of $a^p + b^p = c^p$ with $p \ge 5$ prime. The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$ has discriminant equal to which of the following?
text: Let $a,b,c$ be a hypothetical coprime solution of $a^p + b^p = c^p$ with $p \ge 5$ prime. The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}: y^2 = x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$ has discriminant equal to which of the following? For $y^2 = x(x-\alpha)(x+\beta)$ with $\gamma = \alpha+\beta$, the discriminant is $16(\alpha\beta\gamma)^2$. With $\alpha=a^p,\beta=b^p,\gamma=c^p$ this gives $16\,(a b c)^{2p}$; after the standard change of variables to minimal model the factor of $2^{8}$ drops out, leaving $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$.

q: Evaluate $\log_{10}\Delta$ for the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ with $a=3,\ b=4,\ c=5,\ p=5$. (No integer $c$ exists with $3^5+4^5=c^5$ — this is a sanity check on the formula only.) Round to 2 decimals.
text: Evaluate $\log_{10}\Delta$ for the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ with $a=3,\ b=4,\ c=5,\ p=5$. (No integer $c$ exists with $3^5+4^5=c^5$ — this is a sanity check on the formula only.) Round to 2 decimals. $abc = 60$, so $(abc)^{2p} = 60^{10}$. $\log_{10}(60^{10}) = 10\log_{10}60 \approx 10\cdot 1.7782 = 17.782$. Subtract $\log_{10}2^{8} = 8\log_{10}2 \approx 2.408$: $17.782-2.408 \approx 15.37$.

q: The prime factorization of the Frey discriminant is a perfect $2p$-th power away from $2$. This forces the Frey curve to be:
text: The prime factorization of the Frey discriminant is a perfect $2p$-th power away from $2$. This forces the Frey curve to be: A prime $\ell \mid abc$ with $\ell$ odd appears in $\Delta$ to multiplicity $2p$, i.e. a perfect square. By Tate's algorithm this gives multiplicative reduction, so the curve is semistable; the conductor $N_E$ is squarefree away from $2$.

q: The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is constructed so that its conductor $N_E$ is divisible only by primes dividing $2abc$. Which property of the discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ forces the conductor to be squarefree at odd primes?
text: The Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is constructed so that its conductor $N_E$ is divisible only by primes dividing $2abc$. Which property of the discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^{8}$ forces the conductor to be squarefree at odd primes? Tate's algorithm: if $v_\ell(\Delta) > 0$ but the curve has a node (not a cusp) after minimal change of variables, reduction is multiplicative, contributing $\ell^1$ to the conductor. The $2p$-th power structure in $\Delta$ at odd primes ensures exactly nodal reduction, giving squarefree conductor at those primes. Think about the reduction type when $v_\ell(\Delta)$ is a multiple of $p$.

q: For the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^8$, compute $v_5(\Delta)$ (the $5$-adic valuation) when $a = 5,\ b = 4,\ c = ?$ and $p = 7$. (Only $a = 5$ contributes a factor of $5$; treat $b,c$ as coprime to $5$.) Enter the integer value.
text: For the formal Frey discriminant $\Delta = (abc)^{2p}/2^8$, compute $v_5(\Delta)$ (the $5$-adic valuation) when $a = 5,\ b = 4,\ c = ?$ and $p = 7$. (Only $a = 5$ contributes a factor of $5$; treat $b,c$ as coprime to $5$.) Enter the integer value. $v_5(\Delta) = 2p \cdot v_5(abc) = 2 \cdot 7 \cdot v_5(5) = 14 \cdot 1 = 14$. This is a perfect $2p$-th power valuation, ensuring multiplicative reduction at $5$.

q: Serre's original conjecture (now Khare–Wintenberger theorem) predicts that every odd irreducible $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is modular. In the FLT proof, which weaker statement about $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ actually suffices to apply Ribet's theorem?
text: Serre's original conjecture (now Khare–Wintenberger theorem) predicts that every odd irreducible $\overline{\rho}: G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ is modular. In the FLT proof, which weaker statement about $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ actually suffices to apply Ribet's theorem? Wiles proves $E$ is modular (as a full $p$-adic representation), from which the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is automatically modular. Ribet's theorem then applies to lower the level. The full Serre conjecture is not needed.

q: Which space of modular forms is matched to an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ by the modularity theorem?
text: Which space of modular forms is matched to an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ of conductor $N$ by the modularity theorem? Modularity: $E$ corresponds to a weight-2 newform $f_E \in S_2(\Gamma_0(N_E))$ with $a_p(E) = a_p(f_E)$ for all good primes $p$. The weight is $2$ because $E$ has dimension $1$ (H^1 is 2-dimensional).

q: Cremona's curve $E: y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2$ has conductor $11$. Its associated newform has the $q$-expansion $q - 2q^2 - q^3 + 2q^4 + q^5 + \cdots$. Independently, one can verify $\#E(\mathbb{F}_2) = 5$. What is $a_2(f_E)$? (Use $a_p(E) = p + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.)
text: Cremona's curve $E: y^2 + y = x^3 - x^2$ has conductor $11$. Its associated newform has the $q$-expansion $q - 2q^2 - q^3 + 2q^4 + q^5 + \cdots$. Independently, one can verify $\#E(\mathbb{F}_2) = 5$. What is $a_2(f_E)$? (Use $a_p(E) = p + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.) $a_2(E) = 2 + 1 - 5 = -2$. Modularity says $a_2(f_E) = a_2(E) = -2$, which matches the $-2q^2$ coefficient of the newform $\eta(\tau)^2\eta(11\tau)^2$.

q: Wiles and Taylor–Wiles (1995) proved modularity for semistable elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$. Who extended this to every elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$?
text: Wiles and Taylor–Wiles (1995) proved modularity for semistable elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$. Who extended this to every elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$? The full modularity theorem was completed in 2001 by Breuil, Conrad, Diamond, and Taylor, handling the remaining cases of additive reduction at $3$.

q: The newform $f_E$ associated to the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x^2 - 10x - 10$ of conductor $N = 20$ satisfies $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_3)$. Given $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3) = 4$, what is $a_3(f_E)$?
text: The newform $f_E$ associated to the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 - x^2 - 10x - 10$ of conductor $N = 20$ satisfies $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - \#E(\mathbb{F}_3)$. Given $\#E(\mathbb{F}_3) = 4$, what is $a_3(f_E)$? $a_3(E) = 3 + 1 - 4 = 0$. By the modularity theorem, $a_3(f_E) = a_3(E) = 0$, meaning $3$ is a root of the $L$-factor at $p = 3$ in the newform's $L$-function.

q: The modularity theorem implies that the $L$-function $L(E, s)$ equals the $L$-function $L(f_E, s)$ of a weight-2 newform. Which analytic property does this immediately grant $L(E, s)$?
text: The modularity theorem implies that the $L$-function $L(E, s)$ equals the $L$-function $L(f_E, s)$ of a weight-2 newform. Which analytic property does this immediately grant $L(E, s)$? Hecke's theory gives $L(f, s)$ an analytic continuation and functional equation for any newform $f$. Via modularity $L(E, s) = L(f_E, s)$, the elliptic curve $L$-function inherits both properties — this is essential for BSD and other applications.

q: The space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ has dimension given by the genus formula. For which level $N$ does one first get a nonzero space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) \neq 0$?
text: The space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$ has dimension given by the genus formula. For which level $N$ does one first get a nonzero space $S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) \neq 0$? The genus of $X_0(N)$ (hence $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(N))$) is $0$ for small $N$. The first $N$ with genus $\ge 1$ is $N = 11$, where $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(11)) = 1$, spanned by the newform $\eta(\tau)^2\eta(11\tau)^2$.

q: Ribet's level-lowering, applied to the mod-$p$ Galois representation of the Frey curve, lowers the level from the Frey conductor down to the squarefree part of $\{2\}$: the final level is $N = 2$. What is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$?
text: Ribet's level-lowering, applied to the mod-$p$ Galois representation of the Frey curve, lowers the level from the Frey conductor down to the squarefree part of $\{2\}$: the final level is $N = 2$. What is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$? The genus of $X_0(2)$ is $0$, and $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(N)) = \mathrm{genus}(X_0(N))$. So $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$: there are no weight-2 cusp forms of level 2. This is the contradiction that finishes FLT.

q: Ribet's theorem is sometimes called the ``$\varepsilon$-conjecture.'' Conceptually, what principle does it embody?
text: Ribet's theorem is sometimes called the ``$\varepsilon$-conjecture.'' Conceptually, what principle does it embody? Ribet proved that superfluous primes in the level can be removed whenever the residual Galois representation is unramified there. Applied to the Frey curve, this strips the level all the way down to $2$.

q: Put the pieces together. Assuming $a^p+b^p=c^p$ (with $p\ge 5$, $abc\ne0$), the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is semistable, hence modular (Wiles), and Ribet's theorem lowers the level of $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ to $2$. Why is this a contradiction?
text: Put the pieces together. Assuming $a^p+b^p=c^p$ (with $p\ge 5$, $abc\ne0$), the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is semistable, hence modular (Wiles), and Ribet's theorem lowers the level of $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ to $2$. Why is this a contradiction? After Ribet, $\overline\rho_{E,p}$ would come from a weight-2 newform in $S_2(\Gamma_0(2))$. But $X_0(2)$ has genus $0$, so $S_2(\Gamma_0(2)) = 0$. No such newform exists — contradiction, and therefore $a^p+b^p=c^p$ has no nontrivial integer solutions.

q: Ribet's level-lowering requires the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ to be irreducible. Why does this hold for the Frey curve when $p \ge 5$?
text: Ribet's level-lowering requires the residual representation $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ to be irreducible. Why does this hold for the Frey curve when $p \ge 5$? Mazur classified rational isogenies of prime degree on elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$; semistable curves with a rational $p$-isogeny are excluded for large $p$. The Frey curve's special form and $p \ge 5$ guarantee irreducibility, which Ribet's theorem requires.

q: In Ribet's proof, one removes a prime $q \mid N_E$ from the level by showing $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is unramified at $q$. Which property of $q$ in the Frey setting makes this possible?
text: In Ribet's proof, one removes a prime $q \mid N_E$ from the level by showing $\overline{\rho}_{E,p}$ is unramified at $q$. Which property of $q$ in the Frey setting makes this possible? The key: $v_q(\Delta_E)$ is divisible by $p$ for every odd prime $q \mid abc$. This means $E$ has multiplicative reduction at $q$, and the Galois representation on $E[p]$ is unramified at $q$ (it looks like an unramified twist). Ribet's machinery then removes $q$ from the level. When $p \mid v_q(\Delta)$, the Galois action on $E[p]$ at $q$ acts through a quotient that doesn't see ramification.

q: The conductor of the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is $N_E = 2^s \cdot \prod_{\ell \mid abc,\ \ell\text{ odd}} \ell$ for some $s \ge 1$. After Ribet lowers the level by removing all odd primes, the resulting level is a power of $2$. Given that $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2^k)) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$, what is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8))$?
text: The conductor of the Frey curve $E_{a,b,c}$ is $N_E = 2^s \cdot \prod_{\ell \mid abc,\ \ell\text{ odd}} \ell$ for some $s \ge 1$. After Ribet lowers the level by removing all odd primes, the resulting level is a power of $2$. Given that $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(2^k)) = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$, what is $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8))$? $X_0(8)$ has genus $0$, so $\dim S_2(\Gamma_0(8)) = 0$. This is consistent with the fact that all levels $N = 2^k$ for $k \le 3$ yield genus-$0$ modular curves, and the contradiction in FLT holds regardless of which power of $2$ the level reduces to.

q: Mazur's universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ of an absolutely irreducible residual representation $\overline\rho : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ represents which functor?
text: Mazur's universal deformation ring $R^{\mathrm{univ}}$ of an absolutely irreducible residual representation $\overline\rho : G_\mathbb{Q} \to \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$ represents which functor? A deformation of $\overline\rho$ to $A$ is a continuous lift to $\mathrm{GL}_2(A)$ reducing to $\overline\rho$ mod $\mathfrak{m}_A$, up to conjugation by the kernel of $\mathrm{GL}_2(A) \twoheadrightarrow \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{F}_p)$.
